I got hooked on Angie Grace coloring books a couple of years ago when I purchased [[ASIN:1484182332 Baubles (Angie's Patterns, Vol. 4)]] and [[ASIN:1481841556 Ink Doilies (Angie's Patterns, Vol. 1)]]. I now own several, and when I saw that a new one was coming out, naturally I had to have it!

Angie has an easily recognizable style: not many sharp angles, lots of "woven" and interlaced ribbon-like elements, a soft smooth feel. I've noticed it mature over the years, and her more recent books really play on these traits. This one is very similar to Balance: a lot of "mandala"-like designs, several square, each with a discernible center (I always start coloring in the center -- that's just my little "quirk"). But it's different enough that you won't feel like you're seeing the same patterns. These are more intricate, with more elements.

Most of the designs are incredibly detailed, so you'll need to use fine-point markers or colored pencils for the best results. Images are printed on one side only, so you needn't worry about bleed through ruining the image on the reverse. I always put a sheet or two of printer/copier paper behind the page I'm coloring to protect the next image in the book. The paper is good quality, and although this is bleed-through with markers, there's not a lot of feathering. Still, a light touch when coloring with markers is always a good idea.

Overall, I'm thrilled with this new addition to my Angie Grace collection and expect to get hours of colorful relaxation with it.

"Whimsical" is certainly the key word here. There are a number of garden and pond scenes with enough detail to keep a colorist busy for awhile. There are also more abstract florals, pinwheels, "mandala"-like designs, birds and butterflies. It's a good selection with a lot of variety.

As with all Creative Haven books, a definite plus is that these are printed on one side only -- so no worries about ruining a picture on the opposite side with bleed-through from markers. The pages are also perforated to allow for fairly easy removal. Paper color is a soft white -- not a "pure" white, but not "yellow-ish" either.

So far, I've used colored pencils and Tombow markers with good results.

Bottom line: another winner from Creative Haven. Good quality, nice designs, lots of variation in terms of level of detail.

The thoroughness of this book is all over the place - I put the Table of Contents below my review and I highly recommend checking it before you buy or you might be disappointed.

A few animals are covered in great detail for an intro "vet tech" book (foxes, rabbits) but others are just lumped together by taxonomic Class (rodents, snakes AND lizards) or Order (birds of prey... who get a very stingey 6 pages for the lot). Some animals get their own chapter but it's only a few pages (bats - 6 pages, deer - 4 pages). There's zero standardization of what info is given - it'd be better if it were either a light overview or an in-depth reference instead of a bizarre and frustrating mash-up. There's nothing for felines, mustelids (except badgers), racoons, opossums, etc. I bought it to reference for Wildlife Rehabilitation but it's just too hit-or-miss to be generally useful.... but I'm sure I'll get some use out of it anyways.

It's worth pointing out (because it's not obvious on the Amazon page) this book is written for the UK, not USA. The UK has *significantly* less diverse wildlife, and they regulate exotics more strictly. Be aware there's different drug brand-names and availability between the UK and US which might affect treatment.

Very irritatingly, my copy is totally missing the sections on hand-rearing mammals and caring for baby birds, and the final Index. It doesn't look like they were removed or anything, just..... never put in. I don't know if I got a lemon or if they're all like that, but as a wildlife rehabilitator those sections would have been nice to have and they *are* listed in the Table of Contents with page numbers and everything, so it's not like they're supplements you buy extra.

Here are the Table of Contents and page numbers. Hopefully it'll give you an idea if this book will be useful to you (pages not otherwise listed are intro pages to the sections - I only typed out actual chapters.):

This collection of epistolary essays (essays written in the form of letters) was collected during the weeks shortly after the November 2016 US Presidential election, a time when those who voted for the losing candidate (who actually won the popular vote but lost the Electoral College) were reeling, wondering if the US in which they grew up had somehow transmogrified overnight into an "opposites" version, where inconvenient truth (reports that accurately portray someone in a negative light) = "fake news," compassion is interpreted as weakness, common courtesy = impotence, and respect for those who have sacrificed all is scorn-worthy. Frankly, a lot of folks were petrified at what loomed on the horizon. Events since then have done little to dispel that apprehension.

Enter Carolina De Robertis, who issued a call for letters to encourage and inspire those who were reeling from the outcome. Divided into three sections: "Roots," "Branches," and "Seeds," the letters cover much territory, addressed to individuals including ancestors (the "Roots" section), present-day individuals ("Branches") and future generations ("Seeds").

The basic message is "All is not lost." It may take awhile, it will require hard work to undo the damage inflicted by the constant stream of invective, falsehoods, scandal, and just downright "meanness" -- making scapegoats out of the most vulnerable members of society -- but there is always hope that American Democracy can be reinstated.

Here's the thing, though -- many of the essays here and the messages they convey could be equally applicable to other seemingly hopeless situations in other parts of the world, no matter the adversaries or political situation. Yes, some of them make specific references to the events of November 2016, but the overall message of perseverance, dedication, and resilience applies to many situations, both personal and universal.

It's worth a read, if only to understand some of the cultural impacts. A strong 4-stars -- I like it.

I love doing crafty things -- intricate thread crochet, hand-crafted greeting cards, coloring detailed designs, knitting, counted cross stitch, diamond painting, etc. But SOMETIMES, I just need something a little "mindless," where I don't have to think too hard or worry too much about counting or dexterity.

These sticker-by-number books seem to work well for those times when I just need to wind down.

This particular book has some nice designs, some with a little detail, some with repetitive shapes/colors, but all easy to do in a short amount of time (usually about an hour). Many are "folk-art-ish" with geometric shapes. The colors range from bright primaries to muted softer "country" tones. A nice enjoyable "doesn't require a lot of thought" pastime.

I'm giving 4 stars because occasionally the pieces don't quite fit into the intended space -- I usually use bent-tip opposing tweezers to place the stickers so it's not a matter of having the angle slightly off -- the piece is just cut slightly smaller (or something). However, those are few and far between. Overall, it's an enjoyable activity when I need to chill.
Can't open one at a time, have to read first novel before the next will open.

If you've never crocheted with thread before, but you've had some experience with yarn and reading patterns, this might be a good introduction to "smaller" crochet.

The patterns are all text (no symbol charts) and are easy to follow. They're also nicely photographed so you can check your progress against the photo. Best of all, the finished doilies are not too large, meaning you can finish them fairly quickly. Of the 9 designs, 8 have 15 or fewer rows, and one has 24 (the largest). They range in size from 9" to 14", big enough to "show off," but small enough not to be overwhelming.

There is quite a lot of variety here, too -- from "lacy" to more a little more dense. There's not a lot of texture stitches in this set -- some popcorn stitches, some picots, lots of cluster stitches, all relatively "flat," but that helps keep the complexity at a beginner/advanced-beginner level.

I like this set of patterns -- they come in handy when I need a quick hostess gift and want to do something unique and special.

I'm a cookbook-aholic. I admit it. When I travel, instead of picking up a tee shirt or mug, I purchase a cookbook from the area. So when I was offered "Paris Picnic Club," I couldn't resist this take on Paris "pop-up" cuisine.

Some of the recipes here call for ingredients that may not be readily available in your typical supermarket. Elderflower vinegar, espelette pepper, chestnut flour, etc. But there are enough that use more common ingredients that this might be a good investment if you’re looking for unique recipes with a distinctly French twist.

As is my usual practice, I prepared three recipes to check recipe accuracy and results. I was able to get all the ingredients for these at my local supermarket in a smallish northwest WA town.

Recipes made:

1. Breton Artichokes — one of my favorite vegetables (right up there with Brussels Sprouts). This version is flavored with white wine or cider (wine for me — need you ask? :-) ), various herbs and spicers, garlic, and lemon zest. I made the recipe as written through the boil/simmer step. I decided to pull two of the artichokes out to store in the fridge for later, and proceeded with the rest of the recipe: splitting the remaining two artichokes in half and roasting in garlic, oil, and lemon. I made a tarragon aioli — super yum!

Even though this recipe is two steps, neither requires a lot of attention. You do some prep, and then the artichokes cook for awhile; then do some more prep and they roast for awhile. Not tricky at all, and worth the extra effort. A keeper!

2. Pecan Crusted Rack of Lamb with Parsnip Purée and Chips — I cut this recipe in half (a 6-chop rack instead of the full 12 chops) since I’m single and I wanted to see if it would work.

The good news: it worked! This is a very nice lamb recipe, not terribly difficult, and very very tasty. The lamb is coated with a mixture of finely-chopped pecans, garlic, rosemary, thyme, parsley, lemon zest, salt, pepper, maple syrup, and olive oil.. You rub the lamb with Dijon mustard, then pat on the pecan mixture. It roasts for 15 minutes to achieve medium rare. One tip: the recipe calls or vegetable stock and the book contains a recipe. If you use packaged stock make sure it’s LOW SODIUM. The sauce reduces a lot and the salt level will be intensified by reducing it. If it starts off salty, it will be REALLY salty by the time you’re done.

I made the parsnip purée, but I didn’t bother with the parsnip chips. They’re deep fried, and it was just a little too much work for a meal I was making for myself. For company, I’ll do the chips. The purée was a nice accompaniment to the lamb, a good substitute for potatoes. Another keeper.

3. Asparagus, Green Garlic, Hazelnuts, and Feta — Asparagus has been available now for several weeks, and I live in the Pacific NW where hazelnuts are available year round. I was able to find wild garlic at a local vegetable stand. That — plus some olive oil, butter, and lemon — is all you need for this dish. Preparation is very simple and fast: toast the hazelnuts and chop. Heat oil and butter in skillet, add asparagus and sauté until almost done. Add the wild garlic (I coarsely chopped mine; recipe doesn’t say what to do with them, but I felt they needed a little prep), and cook for just a few minutes. Last thing in the pool is the lemon juice. Serve topped with crumbled feta and the chopped toasted hazelnuts. Yet another winner.

So -- all three recipes I tried worked, but I was pretty careful to select recipes that called for ingredients I knew I could obtain locally, and for which I had the required equipment. This is probably not a book for novice cooks. You need to be reasonably well-versed in basic cooking techniques (for example, the last step of the parsnip purée recipe says to "purée the parsnip mixture," but doesn't say how. You're expected to know this.)

That said, I still give it 4 stars -- the recipes I tried were unusual takes on familiar flavors, and I like that in a cookbook.

I'm not a New Yorker (I'm Kansas City born and raised), but I've visited numerous times and always make a point of trying new eateries, from fancy to street vendor. And I don't consider myself a food snob by any means, but I do love to cook and avidly collect cookbooks (and read them like novels). I guess you could say I have a passionate interest in food and eating. So when this showed up as a selection, I grabbed it. What's better than getting the "insider" perspective straight from the folks who are living it?

I wasn't disappointed. I loved this book!

Of course, the first thing I did was to check to see if there were any chapters on the restaurants I had sampled. First to stand out: Peter Luger Steak House, just off the Williamsburg Bridge in Brooklyn. I've eaten there 2 or 3 times over the years (hint: plan ahead -- they're often booked 6-8 weeks in advance), so I was curious what I'd discover that I didn't already know. Amy Rubenstein, the daughter of the late Sol Forman who bought the restaurant at auction from the Luger family in 1950, gives a straightforward accounting of what it's like to run one of New York's premiere steak houses the "old school" way: no computers, all meat hand selected, and nearly the same menu as the original. As she puts it, "We're a conscious throwback from the fifties, and that's the way we like it." Apparently, so do their customers.

This is the kind of book that you can read a bit at a time. Each chapter is relatively short and self-contained and covers all aspects of the food scene in NYC: restaurant owners, caterers, servers, managers, each with his or her own story to tell. Stories from folks like David McQueen, a waiter at Gramercy Tavern on E. 20th St in NYC's Flatiron district who advises that anyone who wants to become a waiter needs "... to acknowledge that waiting on tables is a servile act and make peace with that. You can wait on your guests with a tremendous amount of self-respect and recognize that, if you do it right, what you are giving to these people they may never get again. It feels good to make someone happy."

The narratives are arranged in themed sections: "Starting from Scratch" profiles narrators who created and built food-centered businesses from the ground up. "Duck and Other Dynasties" deals with food businesses that have been family owned, often for several generations. "Taking the Heat" includes narrators who do their work "behind the scenes" in the kitchen -- in this case, specifically, the line cooks, some of whom are paying their dues on the way to becoming chefs; others who are content with the adrenaline-rush involved in getting the food out each service. "Crowd Feeding" covers institutional food, with narrators that include the food service operations manager for NYC Dept. of Corrections - Rikers Island, and the owner-operator of a "craft service" - catering to film crews on location shooting a film.

I actually liked this more than I thought I would. Don't be intimidated by the possibility of unfamiliar terminology -- there's a good glossary and "The Kitchen Brigade" - a section that briefly describes each of the positions involved in the restaurant business. I knew some of them (hey, I watch "Top Chef" and "Iron Chef America"), but this really helped put things in perspective, especially as it pertains to the narrators.

If you're at all intrested in the lives of the people who feed you, you won't be disappointed. Highly recommended.

I expected a more profound take away. Yes, it is a very quick, simple read. You will have it completed under and hour even with slow, thoughtful reading and reflection. I have a huge library of leadership books and some include allegories and fables. They have their place., and can be phenomenally helpful. It is a way to introduce a complicated, messy topic into manageable, understandable bits of information. Often the story style can leave long lasting mental bullet points as the images combine with the information to provide salient take aways. Easy to remember and draw from as needed. HOWEVER, I did not experience that with this book. You might, and if you elect to buy this book hopefully you get something out of it.

I thought this fell short. I truly expected more from it. I thought the title clever, and since it is touted as "a fable about workplace communication and coming together in a crisis", that it may be EXTRA relevant to what the world is going through with the current pandemic and economic situation. Even outside current events, I still expected a deeper and more informative read even when presented in simple, children's book format.

So what you do get:
-- Oddly described and strangely illustrated characters. I couldn't identify with any of them, and really couldn't identify anyone with them.
--63 pages of story.
--a section on insights as well as a section of questions for "reflection and discussion".

The primary take away is communication and knowing your employees. You need to be direct and clear in what you say and how you say it. Your employees need to feel valued beyond what they are producing. Basically find what the employee responds to that assures them that you care. Go to where they are, not have them come to you. If you are aware of the 5 love Languages, or any of the personality inventories (Johari, DISC, MBTI) or other assesments such as Color Personality Tests, then you know all you need to know and more-- as this book/story barely touches it (yet is clearly based on it).

I am trying to figure out who this would be good for. I won't say there is no value, it just doesn't warrant the price (in any offered format), nor the hype in the praise section. Perhaps if you rarely read- need something quick and not too deep--or if you are very new at leadership and management, then maybe this will be eye opener, a springboard into wanting to learn more. That's it. It would also be ok as part of a small group homework-- read it, get together and discuss it. Try to get as much out of it as you can beyond the story. Discussion in real time may be the most beneficial aspect. The book is the catalyst. Again, I still think it is lacking, and don't really recommend it.

This review is of the audio version of the book.

I was disappointed in this book. There were so many sub plots going on that it made the story seem disjointed. Also, there were too many instances of convenient coincidence - main characters just happen to be in the same place at the same time - even though one of them is randomly traveling around the country? Please. The narrator, Dylan Baker was also annoying. At a number of times, he would assume the character of an English news reporter and speak with a horrible accent for about 5 minutes. I found myself fast forwarding through these segments as they were unbearable. Also, you'd think the production company or Baker himself would bone up on the pronunciation of specific words in the book. He mispronounced Bill Maher's name as may-er, not mar. You'd think he'd know how to pronounce the name of a well known celebrity. Crichton also seems to have a knack for creating characters that are dumb as a rock. Constantly whining and unable to see what would be obvious to anyone else. I'd expect this from a 7th grader trying to pad an essay with extra verbiage, not a writer of Crichton's caliber.

A useful text, but to be clear this is NOT meant to be a comprehensive reference book about wildlife rehabilitation. This publication (its a 3 ring binder with inserts) is better described as a collection of editorials about topics in wildlife, and the actual reference material is minimal, though useful where it does appear. This book isn't meant to be a guide for situations where the rehabber finds him or herself dealing with an emergency or species to which they're not accustomed, or a collection of scientific, veterinary, or taxonomic information (which is what I was hoping for). This is more for "getting your feet wet" and seems to be geared towards someone *considering* entering wildlife rehabilitation than towards the experienced rehabilitator (or animal professional) who needs a text to reference to fill in holes in their experience or to double check their knowledge.

Because it's basically a collection of essays, it can be very difficult to locate the information you need-- or to even tell if it's in the book at all-- within the text. A given essay on a topic may or may not touch on the info you're looking for, and typically doesn't go into sufficient depth for practical application.

It does offer a lot of good tips for legally establishing yourself as a rehabilitator and opening a center/fundraising etc along those lines, but most topics on actual rehabilitation of wildlife are remedial at best. Typically the reader is advised to just take the animal to the vet, to another rehabilitator, or to euthanize it (or rather, have a vet do so). However in the real world those options aren't always available, especially in emergency situations. This text will unfortunately be of little additional help if a rehabilitator needs a quick run-down on how to handle a less-than-ideal situation. Most experienced rehabilitators will find this text of only minimal value, at best.

I completely understand why the NWRA doesn't want to enable lay people without the proper experience or supervision to start billing themselves as rehabilitators or to think they're capable or rehabilitation simply because they purchased a book, and that's definitely the perspective from which the text is written. However, as an experienced rehabilitator and a professional in an animal husbandry industry, I feel there's great need for the kind of reference that this text misleadingly bills itself as. As it stands I've gotten very little use out of this purchase and was overall very disappointed with the depth of material.

I just got back into doing thread crochet after an almost 10-yr hiatus, and this was one of the first pattern books I purchased. I love textured and challenging patterns, and these definitely offer that in spades. Mary Werst's style is somewhat reminiscent of that of Patricia Kristoffersen, whose patterns I used to make regularly. The patterns here go in a slightly different direction, with not as much use of "pineapple" designs (although there are some, including the one I'm currently working on).

Each pattern name starts with the letter "E": "Excellent," "Exclusive," "Extravagant," "Exquisite," you get the idea, fitting for a book titled "Extra-Special Doilies." I'm currently working on the one titled "Extravagant" which is pictured on the back cover, using [[ASIN:B004W8V5S2 Bulk Buy: Aunt Lydia's Crochet Cotton Classic Crochet Thread Size 10 (3-Pack) Peacock 154-856]], a really intense dark peacock greenish blue. I've done similar projects in the past, but not recently, so I'm taking my time to get the texture stitches correct.

These patterns are NOT for the beginning thread crocheter -- you really do need some solid experience following complicated patterns, especially since the patterns are all text (no symbol diagrams). Also, the special stitches needed to achieve the textures are not illustrated, but they are explained at the beginning of each pattern, and the photographs of the finished doilies are very clear.

I really do love this book -- I've already picked out thread that I will use to make 5 of these designs, and there's really only one that I probably won't make ("Exciting") simply because the style just isn't my taste.

The popularity of coloring books for grown-ups is on the rise, and with it a flood of new coloring books and authors, some good and some not-so-good.

I'm happy to report that this is one of the good ones. You get 20 designs in a variety of styles: mandalas, overall patterns, butterflies, florals, some whimsical, some abstract, many featuring the artist's "zentangle" roots. Some are extremely detailed; others offer more "wide open spaces," so whatever your mood, you should find something that appeals. For the beginning coloring artist, this book will allow you to start with some of the less detailed designs and work up to the incredibly detailed ones.

The designs are printed on one side only (YAY!!), with an inspirational quote on the reverse. This means you can use any coloring medium you choose -- alcohol-based markers (BIC Mark-its, Sharpies, Copics, Spectrum Noir, etc.), water-based markers (Crayola SuperTips, Tombow DualTips, etc.), pencils, gel pens, etc. The paper is the usual "CreateSpace" quality, which, in general is pretty good. Still, you'll want to put a piece of scrap paper behind your page if you use alcohol markers to prevent bleed-through to the next page. I use a piece of ink-jet printer paper and haven't had a problem. The pages aren't perforated, so you'll need to take care if you want to remove a picture for framing. I just use a metal-edge ruler and an Exacto knife -- no problem.

I notice that the title says "Volume 1." I'm really hoping there will be a "Volume 2" in the not-too-distant future. Highly recommended!

Of the 126 pages, I found the initial 33 pages the most interesting and helpful. This section is a primer on working with colored pencils, distinguishing between the different types (artist, regular, oil based, wax based, water soluble, etc), supplies and tools, shading techniques, color wheel, blending, burnishing, and layering techniques. All helpful information whether new to drawing or looking to brush up on a forgotten skill. If you are current on your skills, then this section would be a skip over. I am not new to drawing or painting, but it has been long enough that I feel like I need some of the elementary aspects of it to be reviewed. So for me, the first section was a pleasant reminder and rekindled my desire to draw.

However, the next sections kinda lose me. The drawing exercises just get increasingly presumptive that the reader already knows how to draw and that there has been no lapse in their skill. So I hope you know how to sketch out a brick exterior with ornate window frames before you start coloring. Or the detail and layering that goes into bringing cats and dogs to life on a page, as well as people. Can you draw a glass bottle or create a landscape with perspective? I might have at one time, but I am sorely lacking. I guess I misunderstood the intent of this book. I suppose I need to order the authors drawing book before I can make use of this?

Even outside of that- say I am up to date on my drawing skills, something seems missing from this book. If I just wanted to use colored pencils instead of graphite or conte, then I would do so. If I was buying a book because I wanted more information on the colored pencil as a medium, I would expect more instruction on the use of colored pencils- less primer and more subject material. Since it assumes one knows how to draw, and it is about the application of color through colored pencils, then perhaps it would have been better to have a greater amount than what is given in the introduction. Make the majority of the book more like the intro, then have some exercises toward the end.

So based on various factors, I don't know who would benefit most from this book. It doesn't quite do it for me no matter which scenario I play out in my mind (ie; beginner, advanced, curious). Again.. something just seems missing. Stand alone, I don't think there is enough instruction. Maybe paired with other book/s. Thinking a lot about it and going over the book several times.. a middle of road 3 star rating would be too generous. I lean more 2-2.5. Here is the

Heather Terrell manages to create a story that bounces back and forth between ancient history and present day in a way that doesn't lose the reader. And what a story it is. A modern-day art appraiser finds what could be a priceless lost artifact, and the story centers around the creation of this relic: the lost Book of Kildare, while simultaneously chronicling the early church's attitudes toward women.

I enjoyed the book and found no fault with Terrell's style. My only criticism has to do with character development. I would have liked a bit more detail on motivations, etc. If I could, I'd give the book 4.5 stars, as the positives definitely outweigh the negatives on this one.

If you're interested in ancient Irish history, the early history of the church, or historical "detective" stories, you'll probably find this a good read.

A nice mix of traditional crochet patterns, some with a more modern twist. The patterns are clear and relatively easy to follow (text, no symbol charts), with good photos of each item.

I just got back into doing thread crochet after a 10-yr hiatus (busy with work, then returned to college after retirement), and this was one of the first books I picked up. The cover pattern is lovely and works up nicely in thread or yarn. I'm working on a set in thread at the moment to use as coasters (using white and shaded yellow).

There are a lot of yarn patterns, but enough thread to keep me busy for awhile.
I adore Odenkirk, but I wasn't feeling this compilation. Odd, funny stories, but I trudged through them rather than eat them up.
This book isn't well written or edited. The information within contradicts itself, and the book is full of errors.

Some of my fondest memories of family vacations involved camping in tents near Lake of the Ozarks in Missouri. I don’t camp much anymore — but I’ve never forgotten how food always seems to taste better when cooked outdoors over a fire.

Coleman has put together a well-organized comprehensive guide to preparing food while camping. It covers everything from what and how to pack, how to plan meals, how to set up camp and protect food from critters, cleaning up and packing up. Lots of checklists and photos and “handy tips” — side bars containing extra shortcuts or ideas for keeping things organized and simple.

I’ll admit it — I got this primarily for the recipes. While I don’t camp much these days, I DO enjoy cooking outdoors. I’m always looking for new ideas for outdoor meals, and this book is full of them.

My usual practice is to prepare three recipes from any cookbook I review, and I’m following that practice here. I used a kettle-shaped charcoal grill with mesquite charcoal for these. I also have a 7.5 qt cast iron dutch oven (a legacy from my mother) which I used for the dessert recipe. So here goes:

1. Campfire Grilled Brussels Sprouts Salad with Bacon and Cider Vinaigrette
Yes, I went there. I love Brussels sprouts, so I always look for new ways to prepare one of my favorite vegetables. In this case, the sprouts are halved and threaded onto skewers, then brushed with a mustard/garlic/oil mixture before grilling over the coals. Once charred and tender, the sprout halves are tossed with a lemon/honey/apple cider vinegar vinaigrette and crisp bacon crumbles. Yum! Why didn’t I ever think to grill Brussels sprouts before now? This is definitely a keeper.

2. Grilled Swordfish Kebabs
I’m really happy with the variety of main dishes in this book — it’s not just ribs and steaks and burgers (although there are some super creative takes on burgers here). I chose this one because I happened to have the ingredients in my pantry/fridge. Plus, I already had the skewers out from the Brussels sprouts dish above. Chunks of swordfish, zucchini, mushrooms, cherry tomatoes, and onions, brushed with a butter/lemon juice/garlic. This is a really simple dish, but the swordfish is key here — it’s firmer than a lot of other fish and won’t fall apart on the grill (I speak from experience here). Good, repeatable, not earth-shattering.

3. Let’s try dessert: Caramelized Peach Cobbler
I have never tried cooking a dessert in a dutch oven over a grill, but in the interest of completeness I felt I needed to for this review.
It was easier than I thought and worked just fine. The recipe calls for canned peaches — I used fresh and it turned out great. You start with a lot of brown sugar and a lot of butter in the dutch oven, cooking until well combined and smooth. In go the peaches, cook for awhile, then top with a batter made from baking mix (like Bisquick), cover, and move to a cooler part of the fire. Bake until done. I was worried that it might burn, so I kept an eye on it by “peeking” once in awhile. It took about 25 minutes. It was good — the kind of dish that would work well at a campsite to make a special dessert.

So there it is — This is a wonderful book for guiding you through meal preparation while camping — especially when you want something more than burgers and hot dogs. Lots of breakfast dishes, sides, soups, mains, desserts. Solid 5 stars — I love it! I’ll be using some of these in non-camping situations.
First book was not impressive and this one was just plain disappointing.

I purchased the Kindle edition of this. The patterns are fine -- the usual Leisure Arts quality, easy to follow, clear. But the Kindle formatting leaves a lot to be desired. Leisure Arts isn't alone in this shortcoming -- many publishers of crochet patterns simply do a file dump to create the Kindle version and never think about how using an electronic copy of a pattern might differ from how we use printed patterns.

For example, finding the corresponding photo for a pattern is not easy. Some pattern books include a clickable link with each pattern so you can quickly navigate to the photo and back again. Others reformat the pages so that a small photo is included on the same page as the pattern. I ended up adding "Notes" via Kindle to each pattern's first page stating where to find the photo for that pattern ("Cover," "Location 47," etc.).

Then there are a few text formatting issues, where in the middle of an instruction, the text turns into
