in the interest of full disclosure, i did not buy this book. however, i read it in the course of about an hour while sitting at the bookstore. you too should read it for free, if at all.

first off, the author has no real love motorcycles, but the neo-harley dentist/accountant scene that the "american" pig iron scene has become. the photos of him and his friends are enough to make me want to avoid any of the rides, bars, rallies, and scenes that the book describes. frankly, dear author, with your stringy long hair, gauntlets, black leather, and overweight motorcycle, you all look a bit light on your toes. and don't bother with the sportsters, they aren't heavy enough, expensive enough, and can actually be ridden.

second, the cavalier attitude about drinking and riding is disgusting. the main point given to not do so is financial. never mind that riding a bike so large that one can barely control it under the best of circumstances is dangerous, add some alcohol to the mix and create a dangerous situation for everyone. please, dear author, maintain also your cavalier attitude about wearing your helmet and do us all a favor.

third, to state that harleys now have a modern engine is wishful thinking, at best. it is still the same 45 degree pig that it always was, prone to shaking itself apart and mismatched power stroke that makes one grind their teeth. even buell, dear author, has gone to a modern rotax engine that puts the harley engine to shame.

fourth, liberal use of the 'F' word does not a biker make. nor does a liberal sprinkling of 'custom' off the shelf, bolt on, chrome trinkets make a custom bike. i've been riding a long time, have completely disassembled my own bike and rebuilt it how i wanted it (and no, it isn't american and its not japanese), and nothing makes me more amused than to see a bunch of middle aged nobodies riding a bunch of off-the-showroom-floor pigs, all dressed in black and looking for all the world just what they are: overgrown boys that finally have the $30K to buy into a scene and maybe missed their exit for masquerade ball. oh, and trucking your bikes to the rallies and/or flying is about your speed. you earned those scratches on your bikes, buddy. gotta love it.

fifth, if motorcycle gangs don't like to be called gangs and/or gang members, they shouldn't behave as such. sorry, but any 'club' that sells drugs, prostitution, guns, and resolves disputes with violence as their main source of income is a gang. it is what it is, get over it.

lastly, the bullet lists were somewhat helpful. lists of things that one needs on the road, things to watch out for, etc., are about the only thing worthwhile in this waste of paper. but you have to look at skull and crossbones instead of proper bullets, oh brother.

do yourself a favor and read zen and the art of motorcycle maintenance. the reading is better, the author actually cares, and the lists are much more useful.

and the 'F' word simply isn't necessary to get your point across.

This is another book, put together by a panel of experts. These books never work.

I would have given this book 3 stars, that was until I saw the Simple Coiled Bracelet on page 84.

The directions claim it should cost under $25 to make.

There is only one MAJOR problem. It costs $179.95 to buy the Pro Deluxe Coiling Gizmo! The simple, cheap Coiling Gizmo can't make long enough coils to make this bracelet. Should I add that LeRoy Goertz, the inventor of the Coiling Gizmo, designed the Simple Coiled Bracelet pattern?

Forget this book, there are a lot better jewelry making books out there.

My mother taught me to crochet with thread when I was 5 years old as a way to keep me occupied and out of the way. Over the years, I've been in and out of the mood to crochet as life happened and interests changed. I'm getting back into it again, and this was one of the first books of patterns I purchased.

"Big" is the operative word here. There are literally dozens of patterns for tablecloths, edgings, bedspreads, and doilies, all culled from thread company brochures from the 1940s-50s. They've been reformatted for the Kindle, however, so reading them isn't a problem. Also, they've retained the photos and diagrams from the originals. Each project is illustrated with a photo appearing either just before or just after the instructions. This is a definite improvement over other Dover Kindle pattern books, where photos and even parts of instructions are scattered throughout, linked by page numbers from the original book that don't exist in the Kindle edition. This one is very well done and totally easy to follow.

Most of the patterns (except for the bedspread patterns) call for threads that are 1) no longer available, and/or 2) smaller than most current crocheters like to use (the higher the "No." the smaller the thread). No worries -- I just use whatever size I'm comfortable with (usually 10 or 20) and follow the pattern anyway. Yes, it changes the size, but since I make mostly doilies, edgings, and small items like coasters, it's not that much of an issue.

Of the dozens of patterns here, there are probably 20-25 that I'll make for sure, and another 10-15 that are interesting enough to consider. The bottom line, though, is that if you're an intermediate to advanced thread crocheter, you'll definitely find something here that you'll like.

This is a nice collection of fairly simple basic recipes -- nothing too strange or spicy -- that would be suitable for an "intro" to cooking for a pre-teen. Jacques Pepin, of course, is a master chef, so the recipes in this book "work." And they use and teach a variety of cooking techniques and knife skills (something to bear in mind if you're using this with children -- supervision is key here).

The recipes are clear and easy to follow with quite a few photos. I made a few of the recipes ("Salmon for Grandma," "Fish Fillets in Caper Sauce," and "Artichokes with Mustard Sauce"). All turned out fine. Just reading through the book, I'd say the "Fish and Shellfish" and "Vegetables" chapters are probably the strongest in terms of creativity and overall taste; the "Desserts and Confections" is probably the weakest.

Nicely put-together, I can recommend this to parents of older pre-teens who want to learn some basic cooking skills and create decent flavorful dishes in the process.
Childlike

you've got to be kidding me.... how is this a color by number?  You can't even see the numbers - I even broke out the magnifying glass just trying to see if I could figure out what the colors were before deciding that instead of reducing my stress level it's elevating it.  I admit I read other people's reviews and thought to myself 'how bad could it really be?' and since nobody uploading any pictures to give me a guideline of how hard they really are to see I went ahead and bought it... anyway, just to help out anyone else who might be thinking about buying this color by number book I took some CLOSE UP pictures of small sections of the design so that you'll have a better understanding of what the issue is... now if you can see them clearly then good luck... and for anyone who I've saved from a lot of frustration... you're welcome!

Overall, I really like the recipes in this book. Many are simple enough that an intermediate-level cook could handle them easily. I wouldn't recommend it for beginners, however. And some of the specialized ingredients will make it difficult for those not near specialty markets. But overall, there are recipes here to suit most tastes.

I always make at least three recipes from each cookbook I review, and this was no exception. I'm single, and several of the recipes lend themselves well to modification to reduce number of servings. So here are my three:

Shaved Zucchini Salad with Lemon and Marjoram: VERY nice! I love the play of the lemon, flowery marjoram and salty romano cheese against the fresh zucchini. I cut the measurements in half to get 2 substantial servings. This is a keeper for sure!

Pan-seared Halibut with Lemon Relish: I live in the Pacific NW, so getting really good fresh fish is never a problem. I've done pan-seared <whatever> a lot using our local catch (salmon, cod, halibut, bass, etc.) quite often. So the novelty here for me was the lemon relish. Oh.My. This stuff is addictive! I can see using this for any white fish, and I may try it on chicken, too. It's a wonderful concoction of lemons, shallots, green olives, capers, a little salt and red pepper flakes, plus olive oil. YUM!

David's Double-Lemon Dutch Baby: I cut the recipe in half and made it in a smaller 8" skillet -- worked just fine. If you're not familiar with a "dutch baby," it's a sort of cross between soufflé and pancake. It rises while it's baking, then collapses into a sort of hollow "bowl" that can serve as a receptacle for all kinds of goodness. This version has lemon in the batter as well as the topping. I added some frozen huckleberries to the finished product and sprinkled with powdered sugar. Another keeper, not difficult to put together, either.

There are several recipes in the Staples chapter that you use in some of the recipes (lemon-infused olive oil, lemon curd, preserved lemons), as well as a chapter of Drinks that look particularly interesting, too.

This is a small book, only 50 recipes, but beautifully put together and photographed. Would definitely make a nice hostess gift or addition to a cookbook collection. Solid 4 stars.

Maguire’s “thing” is giving us the back story to the well-known fable, and his “Wicked” was a masterful telling of “The Wizard of Oz” from the perspective of the Wicked Witch of the West — and my introduction to this author.

I’ve read most of his later works, and they’ve been a mixed bag: “Confessions of an Ugly Stepsister: A Novel” (a take on Cinderella) and “Mirror, Mirror” (Snow White) were quite good; others, not as successful. The later Oz books just seemed forced, and “Lost: A Novel” was just a hot mess mash-up of too many characters from too many different totally unrelated stories.

This one is somewhere in between. This time out, he stays more or less with one story line: when Alice went down the rabbit hole, what did the people who knew her DO? I’ll admit to being a HUGE fan of Lewis Carroll’s work (as a composer, I’ve set a couple of his poems to music for choir), and Carroll has a particular style and rhythm to his writing. Maguire has clearly made an attempt to emulate that style as much as possible with witty prose, his choice of language, and rather creative plot twists. You don’t just skim this; you really do have to read it in order to get the story Maguire is telling.

The story itself centers around Ada, Alice friend, and Lydia, Alice’s sister, and their efforts both below ground (Ada’s story) and above ground (Lydia) to find the missing Alice. Maguire spends almost no time at all talking about Alice — we all know her story — so we get an alternating “above ground” and “below ground” account of what is going on, including many references to the surrounding physical environment of Oxford and the people and things that were important at the time (Darwin, Debussy, etc.). Maguire does wrap it up at the end, where the two worlds connect, but until then, we get what is actually two stories (three ​if you count the original Lewis Carroll work).

I liked this more than I disliked it. I did find it a little slow going at times, but I truly loved the style of writing. Maguire doesn’t exactly nail the Lewis Carroll style, but he comes pretty darned close.

A solid 4 stars from me.

If you were to drop dead tomorrow, would your family know what to do about all the bits and pieces of your life? Your insurance policies, your bank accounts, your real estate holdings or leases, your online presence including passwords, your taxes, retirement accounts -- the list is likely longer than you think. Yes, an unpleasant thought, but one that we all will face eventually. How prepared are you for that day when you can no longer manage your affairs or, as difficult as it is to contemplate, that day when you're no longer living?

I'm at an age (65+) where I need to make sure I have everything in order and that I have an accessible record for my family to use. In my 30s, I wouldn't have given this two thoughts. But I find it staring me in the face, and I need to get on the ball and get things in order.

"Get It Together" is a large comprehensive book that outlines all the bits and pieces of your life that SOMEONE will have to untangle when you're in a position where you can no longer do so OR after you're gone. The last 1/3 of the book (~150 pages) is nothing but forms that you can use to record everything that your survivors will need to know, including who to contact and when, biographical info (useful for writing an obit), employment, business interests, assets & liabilities, important contacts for health care and finances -- basically EVERY bit of info that they'll need to sort out your estate.

These forms are also available online so you can download and complete them electronically, then either print them and store in a binder, or store them on a thumb drive for use when the time comes.

This is NOT a quick checklist of stuff -- it's an exhaustive compendium of hows and whys to organize everything that is important. I thought I was pretty savvy about this stuff, but there were several things I hadn't considered (car(s) and car insurance/taxes, volunteer organizations in which I hold office where I have a fiduciary responsibility (treasurer), etc.). Heady stuff, but necessary to document.

This is the kind of thing that will take some time and effort to complete, but once completed, it's a matter of updating those bits that change over time. As difficult as it is, your family will thank you.

I've looked at several books like this, and this is by far the most comprehensive. The availability of downloadable eforms pushes this one into 5-star territory.

Yuck! I'm a huge sci fi fan, and this was so-o-o-o disappointing. Black and white photos where awful when representing these types of movies where the set was half the fun. This needed to be in color. A lot of the photos are head shots of the stars that were not even from a scene in the movie. I don't understand the good reviews.

I’m not a regular listener of the “The Splendid Table” radio program although I’ve heard of it and have one other Splendid Table cookbook, but I am a cookbook junky. So I was curious to see what the authors’ take on “weekend cooking” might be.

The recipes are interspersed with essays and commentary about the authors’ creative process, histories of some of the dishes, specifics about various ethnic cuisines and ingredients. I enjoyed this aspect a lot — I’m always interested in how and why cooks develop their recipes and menus. There’s a lot of good info here. But the bulk of the book focuses on recipes.

The first thing you need to know about the recipes in this cookbook is that they are NOT quick-fix easy dishes for those times when you have to get dinner on the table fast. No, these are recipes meant to be pampered, cared for, and nurtured over some hours — lots of hours in some cases. From the introduction:
“What is eating weekends? We think of it as the luxury and the pleasure of taking the time to make things from scratch because they taste better than anything found on a supermarket shelf. It’s about spending a lazy afternoon in a new neighborhood where maybe we don’t speak the language, but can find new markets and restaurants. It’s about the excitement of weaving ourselves into other cultures, and bringing them to our tables with our own hands.”

So if you’re looking for quick and easy dishes, you’ll likely be disappointed in this book.

However, if you’re looking for recipes that require some time and care and you really really love to cook, this might work for you.

I’m somewhere in the middle. I like to cook, but I’m single and don’t usually want to take that much time and effort on a meal just for myself. I did make several of the recipes just to test them out. Here are the results:

1. Moroccan Harira Red Lentil Soup
One of the less-complicated recipes, this one uses fairly basic supermarket ingredients and readily available spices: onion, carrot, parsley, cilantro, garlic, ginger, turmeric, cinnamon, red lentils, paprika, canned tomatoes, and vegetable broth.
I make this recipe as written — no substitutions — and it had good flavor and was easy to prepare. There are a number of “accompaniments” that are listed for serving — dried fruits, phyllo pastries, lemon wedges — that I chose to forego since I was cooking just for myself. But their addition might add a degree of festivity to what is a fairly basic (but tasty) soup. I froze the leftover for later, and it does freeze nicely (defrosted and heated up one serving so far, and it was fine — no loss of flavor or texture). I’ll make this again, and I may use it as a base for adding chicken or lamb to make a more nutritionally robust soup.

2. Charred Lamb with Smoked Romesco
Another dish that’s not quite so complicated, but still packs a flavor punch. I followed the recipe as written. The lamb preparation is fairly simple: leg of lamb cut into chunks and tossed with oil, salt, pepper and a little dark brown sugar, then grilled or roasted (I chose the oven roasting method). The sauce is one I think I’ll use again for grilled chicken: oil, bread, almonds, garlic, red pepper, smoked Spanish paprika, tomatoes, sweet red pepper (roasted), and sherry vinegar, all put through a blender to make a smooth sauce.
Yes, some of these ingredients might a bit tricky to find — we have one local supermarket that seems to specialize in more international ingredients (especially spices), so I was able to get what I needed at one store. The preparation isn’t tricky at all, and the results are quite good. I had leftovers and they were still good the next day (reheated the lamb; the sauce is served at room temperature).

3. Farmer’s Market Pasta
My CSA (consumer supported agriculture) delivery included a couple of zucchini and a fresh Italian chile pepper, so doing this recipe seemed like a no-brainer.
Not tricky at all — I did cut it in half since that’s all the zucchini I had, but it still turned out just fine. I used fettuccine as the pasta, and I had fresh basil on hand (probably the last of my home-grown basil crop - I think it’s about finished for the season). The sauce is garlic, onion, lemon juice, the chile, and a little salt (and optional anchovy fillets — not a fan of anchovies). You make the sauce, cook the pasta and add the zucchini to the pot of pasta when there are 4 minutes left for the pasta to cook. You mix some of the pasta water along with fresh herbs and oil into the sauce, then toss everything together.
This was good — really good — and could be made with just about any vegetable besides zucchini (butternut squash, broccoli, parsnips, carrots, asparagus, etc.). Will make this one again.

So there it is. All the recipes that I tried produced good results with not *too* much effort. But here’s the thing: MOST of the other recipes take a lot of time and energy to produce, with long complex ingredient lists, multiple steps, and hours — even days — of prep and cooking time. And there are very few pictures, so if you're a cook that depends on photos to tell you how a dish should look or how it should be plated, you'll be disappointed. Only you can say whether the ends justify the means. I’ll probably use a few of the more complex recipes for very VERY special occasions for family/friends. Otherwise, this is a book that will likely remain in my “overflow” bookcase of books I seldom reference.

Can’t really justify more than 3 stars for this one, I’m afraid. It’s OK — I don’t dislike it, but I won’t be using it enough to warrant a 4-star “I like it” rating.
One of my favorites!
not an easy read

I was a little apprehensive about ordering this. I was envisioning yet another collection of recipes using mostly processed products: a can of this, a jar of that, with only a little in the way of fresh veggies/etc.

I'm happy to say my apprehension was totally unfounded. This is easy cooking "done right." Using mostly fresh ingredients -- you know, thet stuff on display around the perimeter of your supermarket: the fresh produce section, fresh meat/poultry/seafood, dairy, etc. -- Shawn Syphus has assembled 80 really tempting recipes that will have you in and out of the supermarket and kitchen quickly. For each set of 5 ingredients, Shawn gives you 3 recipe options. They're not just slight variations on a theme, either; each recipe is different in style and seasoning. The "five ingredients" refers to the primary ingredients; you're still expected to have a few staples in your pantry, but Shawn spells that out clearly in the introduction.

The book is laid out nicely, easy to read, with excellent photos of most of the recipes. The steps in each recipe are clear and concise, and all should be doable by even a relatively inexperienced cook. Nothing too difficult or esoteric here.

So how are the recipes? Well, I've tried 3 so far (my standard practice for any cookbook I review), and they all turned out great! First was a yummy Bacon-wrapped Scallops with tomato-basil salad -- perfect for these warmer summer days. Next was a Greek-inspired pasta salad made with pasta (I used rotini), olives, feta cheese, artichoke hearts, and tomatoes -- another summery winner. And finally a chicken dish: Grilled Chicken with Loaded Smashed Potatoes (chicken breast, baby red potatoes, jack/Colby marbled cheese, broccoli, and bacon). Super easy grilled chicken breast with "smashed" red potatoes loaded with broccoli, cheese, and bacon.

There are meat, poultry, seafood, and veggie recipes here, all intended as main dishes, and a separate chapter on desserts. This is where a few of the more convenient "convenience" products appear (boxed cake mix or brownie mix, for example). But overall, this is an excellent collection of relatively easy recipes that should produce good results based on my experience with them.

It's painful to try and read this book, it was either written by kids or someone who doesn't speak English- those are the only explanations I can think of because the things in this are just STRANGE and not really funny. One of the annoying things it mentions is a 6 year old and a 5 year old kid of theirs kicking each other (Very specific, not relatable for most of us) In fact- most of the "annoying" things in this book are specific to the author of the book and the whole book isn't relateable. It's truly terrible and I don't know whether to throw it away or donate it because the thought of some poor soul spending money on it and then reading the junk makes me sad. I'll probably just toss it in the trash.

I'm a fairly fast reader, especially if the book is well-written and holds my interest.

It took me almost 6 months to finish this book. It was VERY tough going. I really wanted to like it, and I kept hoping it would get better, but it never did.

A handful of the many problems:

1. The main character just isn't likable. At all. She's annoying, and the author has made her even more so by specifically laying out her southern accent (albeit inconsistently). Frankly, by about 1/3 of the way through the book, I just didn't care any more about her.

2. The story jumps around like a frog on speed. Just when I think I understand where we are, we shoot off in a totally different direction. That's bad enough, but then we never return to finish what we started in the first place. I HATE that.

3. The recipes are decent, some actually quite good, but they're no better nor worse than you would find in a good cookbook. In other words, don't buy this just for the recipes.

4. The main character just isn't all that believable. If she's so smart, how come she makes so many bad choices?

I think this was a good idea, but the end result doesn't live up to the potential.

What a delightfully quirky but exceptionally informative book! I've never lived in Portland, but I've visited there many times, usually on business (I once worked for a company with a major presence in Aloha and Hillsboro, just west of Portland), so I'm somewhat familiar with the city and surrounding area.

This is truly a "cultural atlas," each page revealing maps showing everything from the distribution of strip clubs to the proximity of urban chicken coops to reported sightings of coyotes. The book is full of historical anecdotes to put the present circumstances in context. Want to see the representation of ethnic cuisines in the food carts Portland is famous for? Check out the "cart-o-grams" on page 144-145. Want to know how to get from one side of Portland to the other while under the least amount of surveillance? Pages 120-121 will show you. How about "scariness level"? The map on pages 74-75 will pinpoint sites of murders, fatal crashes, and "creepy old buildings," with a scariness level of "eyebrow raising" to "hair raising."

Really a fun read, very informative, put together by two geography professors at Portland State University. I loved it!

... combined with a lot of narrative.

I already have quite a collection of "healthy"-recipe cookbooks. This one has some nice recipes -- the Curried Chicken Salad with Green Peas is quite nice (it has mango in it -- one of my favorites), as well as the Pork Chops with Maple Glazed Apples and Polenta -- filling, tasty.

You don't get a huge number of recipes here (fewer than 100), but they seem to be tasty and fairly easy to put together. I think the focus here is really the "Diet Plan & Inspiration" rather than the recipes.

Just buy the actual book, please. Price doesn't always go into my reviews as we have all have individual working budgets. But I have to say, the information contained in this summary at $7 is way way WAY too expensive for a 30 page, large print (not fully utilized) "summary". Had it been more detailed like a Cliff note, or provided a time line, or a list of characters, then maybe I would find it worthwhile. But is a a super generalized gist of each chapter.

It's... ok. It isn't rubbish, but it isn't as telling as it could be. It reads too generically. At best, it might refresh the story for someone who has read the book. It may even give a glimpse into the story for someone who hasn't read it, but is considering it. BUT that same $7 could go toward just buying the book-- and the book is AMAZING. SUCH a well written, super disturbing, true crime, page turning story. In fact, I have purchased the book twice, brand new- one when it came out for me, then I lent it out. Then when it never came back to me, I bought another because I referred to and recommended it so often. I love having it in my library. Such disturbing history told in spellbinding manner.

That being said, I desperately wanted a summary book. I had even mentioned something like that in my 5 star review for the book itself- how it is so informative, and that it is such a tortuous story, that having a summary book to refer back to would be incredible. So when I saw this come out, I thought GREAT! And I bought it despite my reservation toward the price. Now that I have read it, and read it in just under 15 minutes, I don't sense being all that refreshed on the subject matter. And had I not read the book yet, I know I would not have gained enough information to be able to talk about the book. When it comes to deciding to read the book or not, just go look at the reviews or use a search in general about it.

Save yourself the $7 on this, and just apply it to the actual book. The actual book soooooo good.

I love Valentina Harper's coloring books, so I didn't hesitate to order her sticker books. And overall, I'm quite happy.

There are a number of nearly page-sized stickers here -- one is 7.5" x 10" -- along with a number of smaller ones, and each sticker includes a 1/8" white border.

I purchased this to see if I could use it for adding images to the hand-crafted greeting cards that I make. Most of my cards are 4.25" x 5.5" OR 5" x 6.5" -- so none of the larger stickers will work. There are some smaller ones, however, and those will work nicely, but I'll probably need to trim off the white border to use them on my colored cardstock or printed designer paper. Not a deal breaker for me, but bear that in mind if you have the same idea for use.

I like these designs and having them in sticker form is nice. Solid 4 stars.

His Publisher released the ONLY TWO sentences in entire book that sounded interesting.  He wants to be loved and thinks he has to love every-body, even if they are the worse acheivers in the history of our country. I can't afford to waste $21 and I did just that.  Also, book's paper looks very recycled.

It is a well known fact that James Wofford is one of the best instructors of our time and a superior rider. That being said, don't expect an advanced treatise on Eventing from "101 Eventing Tips". The majority of the tips are basic at best, often downright obvious (eg: "Follow the Rules"). That is not to say this book doesn't have its place or wouldn't be valuable to many riders, but with most tips only a couple paragraphs in length, there is little depth to the material.

I have never read anything from the "101 Tips" series, and was hoping to find some technical advise on difficult Eventing "questions" (especially in Cross Country, the discipline unique to Eventing), or suggestions for improving my show-day performance which can only be learned after years of experience. While there were some very helpful tips, I would say by and large most of the information is fairly obvious to anyone who has Evented before, or perhaps even just shown before.

I do believe that this book would be valuable to the right rider: a beginner to horses who is thinking about taking up Eventing might find these tips invaluable. However, for the experienced (even mildly so) rider, I would recommend you take your money elsewhere (for example, Mr. Wofford also has an EXCELLENT book on gymnastic exercises).

I was also surprised to discover this book is undersized (I had envisioned a large book from the picture, but it is actually only about 4 or 5 inches long at the spine), and has no color pictures. I was able to read it in less than 2 hours (it may have been even quicker than that, as I was reading it while doing other things). To summarize, this book would be helpful for the beginner interested in Eventing, but don't expect anything truly life-altering. If you have been Eventing before, this book will probably not give you "that edge" that you're looking for. For the intermediate to advanced rider, you will get only paltry value from this book and may be better served looking elsewhere.

I spent most of this book waiting for something to happen, waiting to spend time with the characters I expected to see. It was only after becomming disappointed that I read on the author's website that this is 1/2 of the original planned book split into two for size's sake.

Okay, that is part of an explanation, but does not really satisfy. That means the next book will not contain any follow through from this one and these story lines will be dangling for several years before being picked up. Worse people who don't know go into this book set for disappointment as they expected to see their favorite characters and were met with a book of new characters and nothing to tell them.

I hope the time between book 4 and book 6 is far less than book 1->4, because spending this much time away from ciritical characters is a sure way to lose interest in a series.
It was on me, that I accidentally bought a German language copy. Once I realized it, I asked to return/exchange and never heard anything back. I really don't know what to do with it now.

Life is too short to read a book that doesn't capture and retain my interest. I started this, put it down. Picked it up again, put it down. I was like.. I really need to finish this so I can review it. Wait, no.. the fact that I am not getting into it IS my review.

I mean, read the story synopsis. Sounds like it should be an attention grabbing fun read, right? It reads awkward, and the style and timing is a little confusing. I think that is primarily why I fade out and feel extra sleepy almost immediately. I am not gloming onto the story or the characters in an immersive way. Bummer.

I do like some of the wording/phrasing.

I may get inspired to finish it off. I hate starting and stopping.. but I am trying to learn to let go and not finish if a book isn't doing anything for me. here is so much out there to read. We'll see. If/when I do, and if I find anything else worth sharing I will come back to do so.

2 stars. Not super terrible, but not doing anything for me, either.

These dot-to-dot books come in two formats -- this is the LARGER of the two measuring almost 12" x 17.5". If you're like me, with older eyes (<ahem!>), you'll be much happier with this size than the smaller one.

Yes, these designs are challenging, but at the same time very relaxing to draw. There are 1000 dots to connect, with each 100-dot segment printed in a different color which really helps keep me on track. For example, if I'm working in the 200-299 section and the numbers/dots are orange, I know I need to look for orange numbers/dots.

I usually do each picture in different color ink, using some fine-tip Uniball Roller Pens. The results are actually almost framable -- and I get a sense of accomplishment when I finish one.

For a relaxing way to spend some time, this book is a good choice. Just get a fine-point pen and start at the star -- one continuous line and 1000 dots later, you'll have something to be proud of.

The book has a big problem in formualing how to go about doing different accounting calcuations. The language is not clear and the diagrams are useless because there is no references as how the calculation came to be. Most of the time it starts on six pages back and if you do not read very carefully, you will miss the whole idea. Had this book for two quarters in college and hated it. All of the students that were in my classes came to the agreement that the authors could not agree and that the exericses were a disaster as well.

If you buy this book and totally understand it Kudos.

Ostwald obviously was upset that Gould turned away from him. This biography is nothing but a shrinks' analysis after being cast aside and feeling hurt. Compared to other biographies, you can tell that after Ostwald was dumped he has no solid facts about Gould. And much of what he writes is contradicted by other biographers who were working with Gould's close friends, letters, and other documents.
This book is a sham, written by a self-absorbed psychiatrist who wanted to make a buck off of his one-time (and short) association with someone famous. Avoid.
If you want an accurate, reliable, well-researched, and very FAIR account of Glenn Gould's life, consider the one written by Kevin Bazzana: "Wondrous Strange: The Life and Art of Glenn Gould."

This is a good quick read. It details, if anything, the ineptitude in the old Soviet system police force.

It is a book about how the most seemingly ordinary and mundane individuals can be the face of pure evil.

What could have been a decent "family lineage"-type of book ended up a sort of mish-mash of historical fact and "fill in the blanks" fictional snippets, not that either is particularly compelling. I suppose if you're a descendant of this particular family, you might find it interesting.

I think the biggest problem I had with the book is its identity crisis: I couldn't figure out if it was supposed to be a serious non-fiction look at a middle-class family history, or a fictional account, loosely based on a few remaining historical records. It took me a LONG time to finish this book -- there just wasn't enough to sustain a "page-turning" interest.

I've been crocheting for over 60 years and I've done more than a few Tunisian crochet projects, so I wondered if I'd learn anything new from this book.

I did. Quite a lot, in fact.

Yes, it covers all the basics, with clear photos showing each step. But where it really shines is when it gets into the more advanced techniques: the stitch variations. The entire chapter "Creating Pattern and Texture" is worth the price of the book for more experienced crocheters. The variety is amazing -- just by combining 2-4 colors of yarn you can create intricate "knit-like" patterns.

I have some really nice baby Alpaca yarn that I purchased on a recent vacation that I've wanted to make into ... something: three shades of gray plus a rich burgundy color. I found just the scarf pattern in this book: page 88 "Textured Scarf" -- can't wait to get started!

Solid 5 stars!

Okay, this book promises good stuff, but ... could it be any harder to understand? You basically need two books to interpret how to make a single pattern. Very vague. We use this book at FIDM and I think it would have been better if the two books we use were put into one. Use it if you must, because it is a good reference for children's and body suits. Also, the illustrations are better than most.

I really wanted to LOVE this book, I really did. I'm such a huge fan of Guy Fieri's "Diners, Drive-Ins and Dives" television show, and I've been hoping he'd come out with a cookbook containing recipes featured on his shows.

I'm a sucker for great road food, and often go out of my way to try a "hole in the wall" diner. Guy has traveled the US highlighting exactly the kinds of places I love to visit. His show on Food Network is loads of fun. No one can describe food like Guy, with his blend of humor and killer adjectives.

The humor doesn't translate all that well in print, and the jokes just aren't that funny. But I didn't get this book for the jokes -- I got it for the recipes.

The recipes that are included are OK -- but if you're a fan of the show, don't expect to find very many of the dishes Guy spotlighted. There are a few (the Cap'n Crunch French Toast from the Blue Moon Cafe in Baltimore, for example), but the bulk of the recipes are "new."

It was more than a little frustrating to read about the wonderful dishes in Guy's descriptions of the restaurant, only to find few recipes for any of them. While each establishment is represented by a recipe, almost none of the recipes are described in the narrative.

For example, Baby Blues Bar-B-Q in Venice, CA -- Guy waxes rhapsodic over the "killer mac and cheese made from four cheeses" and "grilled corn with chipotle-poblano butter and cotijo cheese sprinked on top." The featured recipe: sautéed okra. Huh?

That's not to say these are not GOOD recipes -- in fact, most of them look pretty darned tasty. And if your expectations don't include recipes for dishes featured on the show, these will be just fine.

As a travelogue, it's probably OK, too. And maybe that was its intended purpose, rather than a "cookbook." Or maybe it can't really decide what it wants to be.

But if you're like me, you might be a little disappointed that the dishes included in the book are NOT the ones that made your mouth water when you watched the show OR read about them here.

While I've been on several cruises, I'm a novice when it comes to Caribbean cruises. I've done the North Atlantic, South Pacific, Alaska, and the Panama Canal, but I've not yet done a Caribbean-specific cruise. This will change in the next 12-18 months as I've started looking at my cruise schedule for 2020-2021. This book couldn't have arrived at a better time!

It's organized nicely, giving you ideas for things you can do during your day in port. I say "day" because most of the time, you'll dock in the early morning (7 or 8 o'clock), and depart in the evening. Not always! Sometimes, you'll have only a few hours -- and that's where a book like this can help as it gives you enough info to determine whether you can fit in a visit to *that* museum or a few hours on *that* beach.

This isn't a general guide book for the Caribbean - it is specifically geared toward cruise travel and things you need to know to maximize your time and experience, including choosing a cruise line/ship/itinerary, when to go, what to pack -- these may be different from a "land-based" vacation to the same places. The advice is general: determine your budget, figure out whether you like huge or smaller ships, understand the usual demographics of the cruise lines you're interested in (Holland America's passengers, in general, are older than, say, Royal Caribbean or Norwegian Cruise line guests; some cruise lines have better programs/distractions for children than others; some cruise lines feature more of a focus on cuisine and luxury than on a quick nibble poolside, etc.). You need to start zeroing in on what kind of experience you want before you get into the nitty-gritty of port excursions.

I've found this extremely useful so far -- it shows you where in each port the ship will dock, how close it is to places of interest, how much walking is necessary and whether it's over cobblestones or sidewalks, etc. As an older adult with some mobility challenges, this info is priceless. Many times, I don't know until we dock whether or not I'll be able physically to get off the ship and explore the local area (I can't walk long distances or maneuver a lot of stairs). There's enough info here to give me a good idea of what to expect -- invaluable in signing up ahead of time for ship-sponsored (or private) excursions.

Lots of photos, a nice colorful layout that is easy to navigate -- I really do like this a lot. Solid 5 stars.

Oh, my. This COULD have been a really nice introduction to coloring mandalas. Unfortunately, it fails in production.

The good:
-- Introduction is informative (with some issues -- see below).
-- Designs are printed on one side of the page.
-- There are over 100 (I counted 111) designs to color with a lot of variety in terms of detail, style, etc.

The not so good:
-- Introduction is in black and white -- no color -- so all the discussion of color is sort of pointless.
-- In the section of the introduction on "Felt-Tip Markers," no distinction is made between alcohol-based markers and water-based markers. In fact, the technique they suggest for "painting" with markers (drawing with the marker on a saucer, then using a damp paint brush to pick up the color and paint with it) will work only with water-based markers (like Tombow DualTips or Crayola SuperTips), NOT alcohol-based markers (like BIC Mark-Its or Sharpies, for example). Given that beginners may or may not know the difference, the author missed a great opportunity here.
-- There's a brief mention of "shading," but no examples to show what it looks like (and without color, it would be difficult to show anyway).
-- The paper used is not good quality, certainly not as good as even some of the more inexpensive coloring books (Dover, for example). Water-based markers and gel pens bleed through almost as badly as alcohol-based markers.

The terrible:
-- Almost ALL of the designs are too large for the page, so you lose a little of the design on both sides but not top-bottom. (see photos below). Nearly every single image is missing a slender "slice" of the outer side edges of the design. The "Look Inside ... " doesn't show this. So if it's important to you to have the entire image to color, you might want to think twice about adding this to your collection of coloring books.
-- It's impossible to remove a page to color without tearing/destroying it, and also nearly impossible to get the book to lie flat on a copier to make a photocopy onto better paper for coloring, which wouldn't do much good anyway since the image is too large for the page and you're missing a bit of the design on either side.

As I said, this COULD have been a really nice book featuring mandalas, their history, and coloring tips/ideas. But given the problems, I can't recommend it. Sorry.

I received this as a "thank you" gift from a choir headquartered in Denver (they performed one of my compositions ... ) -- I collect cookbooks and especially like getting regional cookbooks that feature dishes and menus that are "local" to the area.

This book features recipes from chefs in the Denver/Boulder area. Regional cookbooks give me a sense of the food traditions in an area, and this is a good example. Each major city or geographic region/area usually has famous "signature" dishes (think Chicago deep-dish pizza, Philadelphia Cheese Steak sandwiches, New Orleans Po'Boys, Texas barbecue, etc.).

So how do you define Denver cuisine?

If this book is any indication, it's a comfortable blend of Mexican, midwestern "home style," mountain specialties like trout and quail, Colorado lamb, and a nice collection of international dishes reflecting the international population (curries, satay, Korean kimchi) that has come to call Denver home over the years.

"5280" refers to Denver's "mile-high" designation (5,280 feet in a mile), and it's also the name of the magazine founded in 1993 to present a take on life in the are. The book includes 118 recipes including 25 recipes for various cocktails. Each recipe was contributed by an area chef or bartender, but they've been streamlined/simplified to make them more doable by the average home cook. There aren't a lot of photos, so if you're a more "visual" cook, you might be disappointed. The photos of recipes that are included are beautifully photographed, and there are a few photos of some of the contributors. The design is attractive and balanced, and the type is easy to read.

So how are the recipes? I always prepare at least 3 recipes from each cookbook that I review, so here are my picks from "5280"?

1. Cod with Pea Purée and Leeks in Cartoccio
First things first: "in Cartoccio" just means wrapped and baked in parchment. So this recipe is super easy and clean-up is a breeze. I can get local cod so I was good to go. The cod is seasoned with lemon and lime juice and marjoram and topped with thinly sliced leeks, then wrapped in parchment and baked for 15 minutes in a 450-degree oven. The pea purée can be made from either fresh or frozen peas (I used frozen) and is seasoned with tarragon. Peas are blanched, then puréed with the tarragon, a little of the cooking water, and some olive oil. Easy, right?
I liked this. It was SUPER easy to make - really, you just get the fish in the oven, then make the purée, and everything finishes about the same time. It's not a super-duper standout, but it IS a good solid recipe and you could change it up by changing the fish and/or herbs.

2. Quinoa and Rice Pilaf
I "discovered" quinoa about 4 years ago and find it's a great addition to my "grains" arsenal. I've collected a few quinoa salads so I was curious to try this "hot" version of pilaf. This has a lot of nice robust flavors: saffron, dried currants, dried barberries (you can substitute dried cranberries or dried sour cherries -- I used the cherries), red and green bell pepper, toasted pine nuts, basmati rice and quinoa. You cook the rice with the dried fruit and saffron, cook the quinoa separately, then combine the grains/fruit with finely chopped peppers (raw) and the pine nuts quickly to serve hot. This makes a LOT -- but it keeps reasonably well for several days and is good cold as a salad, too. I might add a little heat (maybe a very finely chopped hot pepper. Another solid recipe, and easy to prepare.

3. I had to try one of the cocktails. Many of them use liqueurs that I don't have on hand (and I wasn't too keen on purchasing multiple bottles of odd bottles that I may never use again, so I found one for which I had the ingredients: "Western Slope" -- a peachy concoction served in a martini glass. A peach slice (this called for a specific brand; I found what I thought would be a decent substitute), whiskey, peach liqueur, honey water (half and half mixture of ... well ... honey and water). Muddle the peach slice in a shaker, add the rest of the ingredients + ice, shake, strain and serve. I'm a sucker for all things peach, and this was VERY good. Keeping this recipe on hand for this summer.

I want to work my way through more of these recipes. There are a few that, even though "simplified," are still quite complex (multiple components that must be prepared first, then assembled in a specific way), so I'll wait until I'm cooking fo rmore than one person to try them. But there are some really great looking side dishes and desserts.

I "like" this cookbook -- I enjoy reading cookbooks and this does a good job of revealing the current state of food at 5280 feet. Recipes are doable with a good variety from very simple to rather complex. Solid 4 stars.
The title and description of this “book” are very misleading. This is a GAG GIFT.  Each page is 99% blank, at the top it alternates saying “inhale” or “exhale.”
This 3rd book - I knew right away who the "villain" was. The other 2 were better

This book had the potential to be so great. I enjoyed it in the beginning but as the book continued, the main character became less likable and her behavior increasingly unbelievable by making many rash decisions that put others in danger. Many events did not seem to fit together. For example- keeping up the appearances of dinner parties and church. Both of these happened once in the beginning but there is no further mention of any further church appearances or parties. It also did not make sense that Anna & the Kommandant would be keeping their relationship a secret but yet they would be having dinner and going to the symphony regularly. Since he was such an eligible bachelor and given his position, if they were on the social scene in public even a few times, the secret would have been impossible to keep.  Anna's anger at the Kommandant for his wife's death was nonsensical. The book indicates he could not save his father in law without putting his wife's life in danger. It seemed like Anna would have understood that and had been drawn closer to him as opposed to farther away. It was a disappointing ending. I do not believe the Kommandant would have shot her, as she & Marta day would have happened. It is much more believable that he would have shot himself when he found out that Anna was Jewish; or, he may have run away with her when he found out the truth. Finally, Anna's “attachment” to her husband did not seem sincere. His attachment to her I also question, with him being so close to her so often and not being able to even give her a little more contact. The book seemed half-baked. It would benefit greatly from being edited and reworked to be a masterpiece, which it has the potential to be.
All this book says over and over is watch his YouTube! Watch his YouTube ! Waste of money ! Oh wait watch his YouTube! Ugh
Nice, but the illustrations are outdated and a little hard to follow.

Gorgeous doily patterns! You get 6 patterns in all for round doilies, each a different style, and each uses size 10 crochet thread and a 1.50mm hook. The doilies range in size from 13" to 18", and use a variety of simple crochet stitches in various combinations. There are a few "complex" stitches (clusters, picots, decreases), but these are clearly spelled out at the beginning of the pattern in which they're used. Instructions are clear, and the photographs serve as a guide as well. I'd rate these as "advanced beginner" / "intermediate" level thread patterns.

If you're brand new to thread crochet, you might want to tackle something a little smaller / less ambitious. But if you have a few doilies under your crochet belt, these might be a nice move up in difficulty.

I already have plans for several of these in different colors of thread. Really a nice collection with good variety.

I have to admit I'm a sucker for this type of historical narrative: the "in search of" genre. Finding the Northwest Passage was considered the absolute gem in late 18th and early 19th century exploration.

Brandt has given us a well-written fascinating account of the effort expended in this endeavor. He clearly conveys the sense of nationalistic pride the British took in their explorations - they had just defeated Napoleon -- and the level of detail is just right.

My only quibble: I would have liked a map or two. I'm more "visual" when it comes to stories like this one.

Still, I highly recommend this to students of history.

I've been crocheting most of my life, off and on, and originally learned to crochet using thread and a small steel crochet hook when I was 5 yrs old. I'm a sucker for lovely lacy doilies -- they fit my personal decorating style (sort of "1900s vintage meets modern eclectic"), and there is a lot you can do with them. It's just so relaxing to start with a long piece of string and end up with something fantastic.

There are SO MANY great patterns here, all of them charted and with full-color photos. I've made the first one (the one in the upper left corner of the cover), and had no issues at all with the charted design, despite the fact that the few written instructions are in Japanese. The chart is very clear, and you have the detailed photo as a guide, too. There are pineapples, "mandala"-type, circular filet, a few ruffles, too. Really, something for everyone.

It appears that most of the patterns call for size 40 thread -- too small for my over-65-yr-old eyes, so I substitute size 10 (or 20) with a size 1.50mm (1.15mm) hook. Seriously, any thread pattern can be made using any thread (or yarn for that matter) with the corresponding hook size. The size of the finished item will be different, but most of the time for doilies, that's not too much of an issue.

My goal is to work through this book one by one, using different types of thread (solids, ombré, variegated, etc.), framing some of them as wall art. This should keep me busy for a LONG time.

(Doily in photos are made from #1 in the book, using size 10 thread and 1.50mm hook. It measures about 14" diameter.)

What a lovely book! Quality all the way through, from the cover (highlighted with copper and silver foil) to the single-sided perforated pages to the hints of color printing highlighting the foliage in the pictures.

The subject is animals in all their glory. The designs have an almost whimsical quality about them but at the same time, ​there's a certain realism, too. You can almost feel the personalities of these animals. These are *extremely* detailed drawings which need sharp or ultra-fine points on pencils or markers, so if you're looking for more "wide open spaces" to color, this probably isn't going to work for you. I'm using Staedtler Triplus Fineliners, and so far, the results are excellent.

The paper is heavy -- almost a light card stock -- and smooth, which means that feathering that happens often with markers is minimized. There are 31 portraits -- "head shots," some face forward and some profile shots -- of both wild and domestic animals. Very creative use of almost "zentangle"-like shapes. These are challenging but rewarding images -- plan to spend awhile on each.

This would be a great addition to any coloring book collection -- absolutely first-rate quality and good variety. Highly recommended!
Why would I buy a notebook for meetings without being able to see what the pages look like? Ridiculous.

Instead, she comes to the conclusion that most people, including first responders and even Yuri Yudin dismissed (though he did think something similar I suppose). Her conclusion does not fit the evidence in the files. I can't elaborate on what is wrong with her solution without giving it away but I I said to myself more than one "no, no no" and out loud at least twice "are you kidding me??!!!" No, I am not an expert on this case and I do not pretend to be but I have read enough books to understand it. True, abduction from beings from the planet Golgofur in the Hipolick solar system (as yet undiscovered by science lol) is about what some of the theories are like and this is abundantly better than those. At least it is in the realm of possibility but it's rather far down on the list of what could have happened, I just don't think it's believable. I gave it 3 stars because it lays out the theory fairly well, though dismisses too much out of hand, it adds to the literature on the case and was fairly well written by a person who does not speak English as a first language.

If you have already read books on the case and are familiar with it, it's ok. If this is the first book you are reading on it, go with "Dead Mountain" by Donnie Eicher first (even though the title itself is misleading). It gives a chronology of the groups timeline a little better, whether you buy into his conclusion or not. It is a good way to get acquainted with the case.
interesting but not great.
It should clearly state not a workbook but lesson idea and plans for kids
This book wasn't for us. I just felt that some of the pictures and passages that were gross. I found that they were objectionable for young children. I did not care for it.
Didn't cost much and you get what you pay for.

I'm a "non-traditional" undergrad (trans.: "older than dirt"), a music major, who is in the process of getting ready for grad school. My math background is pretty limited: I haven't studied algebra or geometry since high school (about 40 yrs ago - 1965-66), and I had some minimal statistics and probability in a "math for non-science/math majors" course a couple of years ago. So it was with some trepidation that I started preparing for the GRE general test. Oh, I figured I'd do OK on the verbal -- living this long, you get reasonably familiar with vocabulary. But thinking about the quantitative portion threw me into a real panic.

I decided I needed help -- and ordered this book based on the description (and price). Note that this is the edition WITHOUT the companion CD. You do have access to the Princeton Review website and the online tools there, and you'll be wise to take advantage of them.

I used this book and the online tools in conjunction with the ETS PowerPrep software. Due to my school schedule, projects, finals, etc., I didn't start studying in earnest until about 4 days before the test. Probably not the smartest move on my part, but it was the best I could do. Before I started studying, I took a practice test just to see how I'd do -- ugh! not very good at all (550 verbal, 450 quantitative). So I spent about 2/3 of my time familiarizing myself with the tricks and tips for the math section offered by this book, and 1/3 of my time on the verbal and analytical writing sections.

The result: a very respectable 630 verbal score, and an astounding 730 quantitative score. The "tricks" offered by this book made all the difference in the math results.

It's not perfect -- you really do need to use the ETS preparation software, especially for the analytical writing part. Also, the ETS practice tests seem to be just a little closer in difficulty to the "real" test, but the PR tests aren't bad at all in that respect. I'm giving it 4 stars only because I rarely give 5-star ratings for things like this.

Some people have complained about the PR level of difficulty, their approach, etc. All I can say is that it definitely helped me nail the math section, and I consider it money well spent. Your mileage may vary.

This is way too different than the original. It just goes off on some weird tangents. It is not what I expected when we got Repunzel. It is definitely not what my kids were expecting.

The physical quality of the book was good. The pictures were beautiful. It is okay, just know you are getting a very different version.
If you want to send to me I will read and review

Joan Hess was NOT the best choice for using Peters' notes, etc to write the last Peabody book. Her style of writing is so different that the characters were unbelievable as the ones Peters fans know. The ideas were great, unfortunately the writing ruined the story. Leave it be & reminisce about the others.

I'm a sucker for coloring books with detailed images. This is one of those books. The images are filled with little details, some hidden images, and a lot of imagination and in some cases whimsy. Fans of Kerby Rosanes will find a lot to like here -- the scenes are a little gentler, a little more "rounded."

The printing is sharp, the paper is good quality, printing is on one side only, and each page is perforated to allow easy removal from the book. I usually use colored pencils for images like these, but I tested out my markers. No bleed-through at all with my water-based pens; with my alcohol-based pens, the only bleed-through happened when I held the tip of the pen on the paper for 10+ seconds. Not usually something that most people will do, so I think it's safe to say bleed-through shouldn't be a problem for most people.

I like this enough to look at purchasing additional books in this series. It's that good ...

I do not care for books that do not have a nice final happy ending as the resolution. I feel like if you pay for a full book, you deserve the chance to read a complete story that leaves you feeling satisfied at the end, not a big cliffhanger that says "please by the next one in the series." The writing is competent, of course, from this multi-published author and it was an okay read. It has a lot of characters and different stories. It does switch from the first to third person point of view depending on who is speaking, which I also didn't care for as much. You can read it without reading the other books, although you can see some of the other characters popping in here and there. It was slow at times. Books that don't have a happy ending, or really an ending at all, are not for me.

I've been buying him the Walking Dead comics in hardcover and assumed Attack on Titan was doing something similar - instead this is a book full of fan based stories with some great pictures. I LOVE crocheting from symbol diagrams ("charts"). They just work better for me than the text line-by-line or round-by-round instructions that take up multiple pages. With symbol charts, you get the entire set of instructions in (usually) a single diagram on one page, and once you learn what the symbols mean, it's a quick easy way to figure out a pattern.

So what you're getting here is a large book with ** 80 ** doilies illustrated with beautiful clear photos AND symbol charts -- a compilation of two separate books ("Crochet Lace Doily" and "Pineapple Lace Doily").

As with a lot of symbol charts, there is some written text that explains the thread and hook and finished doily sizes as well as some detailed instructions about some of the stitches. Unless you can read Chinese, this really won't help you much. HOWEVER, don't be deterred: I'd say that most of the designs here are accessible for any crocheter who is already somewhat familiar with symbol charts (if not, you probably should start with something where the auxiliary instructions are written in English or your own language ... ). That said, there's a very nice visual/graphic at the end of the book showing each symbol and exactly how it should be stitched, again with auxiliary text in Chinese, but certainly clear enough to figure out from the drawings. For me, between the clear photographs and the symbol charts, I think I'm good with almost all of the designs. I'll just use my usual size 10 crochet cotton and 1.50mm hook and plan on the finished item being larger than described in the text. It appears to recommend size 40 thread and a size 8 (0.90mm - 1.00mm) hook based on international sizing standards (Clover, addi, etc.); doily dimensions are given in cm, so a 34cm diameter doily would be a little under 13.5" -- with size 10 thread and a 1.50mm hook, we're probably looking at 15-17" when finished.

The first half of the book "Crochet Lace Doily" features a lot of filet crochet designs -- both square/rectangle as well as hexagon or octagon shaped -- and some "motif" doilies consisting of several "patches" stitched together. The second half "Pineapple Lace Doily" is just what the title says: a collection of really nice designs featuring the "pineapple" figure (teardrop shape small scale-like "loops", etc.) in all shapes and configurations. This section alone is worth the price of the book for me. So many lovely designs -- so little time!

I'm totally happy with this book and feel it's well worth the investment. It's one I'll use time and time again over the next several years. Solid 5 stars from me.

If you're interested only in the east side of south Florida, the Everglades, and the Keys, this book will work nicely for you. But if you're interested in the WEST side of south Florida, you'll be disappointed.

I got this specifically because I'll be visiting Florida in October of next year at the end of a Panama Canal cruise that docks in Miami. My plan is to take 7-10 days exploring south Florida by rental car, and then fly home to the Pacific NW.

Unfortunately, I'll definitely need another guide to check out Naples, Marco Island, Fort Myers, Sanibel Island -- basically, anything on the Gulf side of the southern part of Florida, since these aren't covered at all other than brief references to things like catching a ferry to the Keys or a place to eat if you're visiting the Everglades.

The good news is that Miami, Ft. Lauderdale, Boca Raton, Palm Beach, the Keys, and the Everglades are nicely covered with a lot of good current info. I've been to Miami several times, so I'm already somewhat familiar with that area, but I'm definitely looking forward to visiting the Keys as well as the Everglades. Lots of good guidance, including some recommendations for "off the beaten path" attractions.

You'll also need to get a good map. The only maps provided in the book are for Miami city and Orlando -- which is in the north part of the state, not the focus of this guide. I really don't understand including it at all!

Others have mentioned the printing, and I, too, found it difficult to read. My plan now is to use this along with at least one or two other guides plus various websites to plan my road trip itinerary and take notes, then leave the book home.

If your need for a guide to south Florida is very specific to just the few south Florida places that are covered here, and your eyesight is much better than mine, you will probably find this book a reasonable source of info. But the lack of a good comprehensive map and its lack of coverage of the west side of south Florida makes it probably not the best choice. Three stars is the max I can give it.

What a nice set of lovely colorful doily patterns! I love the combinations of colors -- the soft rusts, aqua, apricot paired with taupe, blues -- and the patterns perfectly bring out the contrast when different colors are used. Most have a floral theme, too -- just super nice.

The difficulty level ranges from easy ("Marigolds" on page 14 and "Forget-Me-Not" on page 30) to experienced ("White Shamrocks" on page 26), with the rest being rated intermediate. However, even the designs rated as "easy" require a knowledge that seems to me to be more "advanced beginner," so keep that in mind. You might want to start with a couple of easier patterns in one of the "how to crochet with thread for beginners" books that are available.

That said, most intermediate crocheters should have no problems with these patterns. They call for size 10 cotton -- fairly standard for doilies -- and hook sizes ranging from 1.5mm to 1.9mm - again, fairly standard hook sizes. Like most doilies, you'll need to block these upon completion. I usually just wet mine down with a spritz of water, then pin it to a piece of foam board covered with plastic wrap, and leave overnight. If I need to stiffen it, I used one of the readily available stiffeners, but that happens rarely. Usually,​ just stretching/pinning/drying does the trick.

Glad I added this one to my collection. It's really nice and I'll be making several more (I've already completed two of the patterns).

First things first: This is NOT a travel guide! If that's what you're expecting, you will be disappointed. Locations are given, but it's up to you to dig deeper if you want to visit in person a particular site.

No, this more about the specific natural geographical/geophysical/geological occurrences that make our planet so interesting, beautifully photographed, with "layman's terms" explanations of the natural processes that produced each phenomenon.

Well organized, it can be useful in conjunction with various travel guides if you want to see the "wonders" described up close and personal. I'm using it for just that purpose. I have a number of cruise vacations planned over the next two years, including the North Atlantic (Labrador, Greenland, Iceland, Norway, etc.) and French Polynesia, and I'm using this book to plan onshore excursions. I find a particular site I want to visit based on this book, and then use a country-specific travel guide to determine if a visit is even doable. If so, I then check to see if there are any commercial tours available. It's making the planning process a lot easier, and I'm finding a lot of natural landmarks that seem to be within the realm of possibility to visit.

Apart from the practical application, it's really a beautiful book. Large full-color photos, decent explanations -- although it's clear that several people worked on this as the styles of writing vary from chapter to chapter. Still, it's the kind of book you want to have handy for a quick flip-through when time is available. It's a large heavy book, definitely of the "coffee table" variety, nicely bound and printed. No flaws there.

I really like it and will continue to use it to plan future vacation explorations. Solid 4 stars.
