What a nice find! I'm an avid cookbook collector and have a couple dozen books intended for the "new cook" or "college student," but this may be my favorite so far.

What makes it a good choice:
1) The author doesn't "talk down" to her audience. She presents tips and techniques in a straightforward way, explaining what to do and WHY.
2) Recipes, although simple, cover the gamut from easy snacks to sit-down dinners with more sophisticated preparation and presentation.
3) Each major chapter includes a "Choose Your Own Adventure" section at the end with tips for taking a basic recipe and customizing it to one's own taste, creating a menu, or fashioning a dinner party.

My usual practice is to make 3 recipes from each book I review, and I did that with this book.

1. Maple Mustard Pork Chops -- a nice basic recipe that serves 2. Really just sauteed pork chops with a yummy maple mustard sauce (whole grain mustard, maple syrup, apple cider vinegar, and sour cream). The pork chops are pan-fried, then served with the sauce. I'll be using that sauce in the future -- it's that good and so simple. I served this with ...
2. Roasted Broccoli with Lemon and Parmesan -- I cut this recipe in half to make 2 servings and so it would fit in my toaster oven. This is a standard "sheet pan" preparation: fresh broccoli cut into same-size pieces, tossed with oil/salt/pepper, and roasted until tender and lightly browned, then sprinkled with lemon juice, finely grated Parmesan cheese, and a pinch of red pepper flakes. Again, an easy recipe well explained, with tasty results.
3. Mix-in-the-Pan Applesauce Cake -- a "from scratch" cake that comes together easily with minimal mess. Using an 8" square cake pan, you dump all the dry ingredients into it, mix with a fork, make a well, add the wet ingredients, and again mix with a fork until blended. Then bake. There's a recipe for a buttercream icing, too. Super easy, and great results.

The point here is that cooking needn't be intimidating, that mastering a few simple techniques can go a long way to producing great food, and that it's OK to shake things up a bit with your own variations as long as you start with a good recipe.

Solid 5 stars from me on this guide for new cooks.

I've used the Lonely Planet guides for years, and they're usually quite good. And I LOVE taking road trips, so anything that helps with planning is a good thing.

This new release from LP is quite good in certain areas, and not so good in others. It's clear there were multiple authors with very different approaches/insights. Some chapters are filled with towns and attractions that are "off the beaten path," or less obvious; others contain the same old tired tourist traps, with no real new and exciting places to go and things to see.

I decided to check out the treatment of places I'm most familiar with: Pacific NW (specifically WA state), California (specifically northern CA), and the Great Plains. I grew up in the midwest, live in CA for 28 yrs, and now call the Pacific NW home.

The good stuff: the Pacific NW section is good, as far as it goes. It doesn't include a lot of "trips," but the included trips are detailed enough, and include enough esoteric info to make them standouts. The Cascade Drive and the Olympic Peninsula Loop are filled with enough detail to keep one occupied for 4 or 5 days (and I've done both of these trips).

The Great Plains trips are likewise detailed enough to spark interest. Again, there aren't very many trips, and nothing, really, in Iowa (where I can personally recommend a day or two in the Amana Colonies), but the ones that are presented are quite good.

The California section was a definite let-down. On the Pacific Coast Highways trip, for example, virtually every "highlight" is a well-known tourist mecca (Golden Gate Park? Hearst Castle? Monterey Bay Aquarium? really? Nothing new here ...). Even the more detailed San Francisco, Marin and Napa Loop contains very little that you couldn't find on the AAA website. I really wanted to see more "discoveries," not the same old stuff that's been written about in popular travel guides.

For the serious road tripper, I would invest instead in more localized guides at the regional, state, or city level. I can definitely vouch for the Lonely Planet guides [[ASIN:1742203019 Lonely Planet Washington, Oregon & the Pacific Northwest (Travel Guide)]], [[ASIN:1741794064 Lonely Planet Yosemite, Sequoia & Kings Canyon National Parks (Travel Guide)]], and [[ASIN:1742207456 Lonely Planet British Columbia & the Canadian Rockies (Travel Guide)]], as I've used all three of these to plan trips. FIgure out where you want to go, and get the local or regional Lonely Planet guide for that area, and I think you'll be happier.

This book works as an overview; not as a detailed trip planner.

I live in northern WA state about 13 miles from the Canadian border and a 45-minutes drive from Vancouver. I've visited both Vancouver and Victoria a number of times, so I was curious to see what Fodor's guide would recommend.

This is a good comprehensive guide -- not too detailed, but it hits most of the "must see" spots in both cities. The two cities are very different, and not just in terms of size. Victoria, the capital of British Columbia, always "feels" more British to me, a little more formal. Vancouver is more eclectic, more cosmopolitan. I like both, but I find myself drawn to Victoria more often.

That said, if you are planning your first visit to either city, this is a good guide to start with. Everything seems to be current -- restaurants, major attractions, etc. -- and the recommendations for what time of year to explore the area is spot on.

I'll be taking another trip to Victoria in a few weeks and plan to check out a few places I haven't yet explored. I always like finding new areas to visit -- and this couldn't have come at a better time. Good stuff, for sure.

I love the look of "sampler" afghans -- and I've made several over the years from various pattern books. Somehow, I missed this one, and it's one of the best I've seen.

Each square utilizes a different stitch design. There are no color changes within a square (YAY! I HATE HATE HATE having to weave in the ends, which probably explains why I've never made a granny square afghan). The cover illustration shows this in soft neutrals, but it would be equally attractive done in soft pastels. The stitch texture would likely be lost in dark colors or variegated or ombre yarn, so keep that in mind. My plan is to make this in shades of pale green and soft yellow/cream for my guest bedroom. I'm also planning to do this in size 3 thread, doubling the width of the squares and making a table runner. I have the thread already in my stash for this and will post a photo when I finish it.

Instructions are clear, photos are clear enough to show detail. No issues at all with the printing. Solid 5 stars: I love it and can see so many possibilities for using the stitch patterns in other things (scarf, shawl, etc.).

If you've been doing thread crochet anytime during the 1980s-present, the name Delsie Rhoades will be quite familiar to you from her numerous designs that were published in many many magazines, books, and pamphlets during that period.

This collection of 16 patterns features 13 by Delsie Rhoades, two by Anne Kirtley (who is also the technical editor for this book), and one by Lucille La Flamme, another familiar name to thread hookers.

As indicated by the title, all the patterns feature the "pineapple" design -- a tear-drop shaped series of loops with (usually) netting or shells between. The patterns themselves show great variety, too -- even though all are based on this pineapple idea. There's one oval pattern; the rest are all circular. Seriously, there's something for everyone here, from advanced beginner to expert.

Sizes range from 8" to 28", and all use "bedspread weight" (size 10) crochet cotton, and either a size 5 (1.8 or 1.9 mm) or 6 (1.75mm) hook. A note about hook sizes -- they've changed over the years, and the editor of this book was kind enough to include the size equivalencies in mm. I'm using a Clover Soft Touch steel hook No. 0 which is 1.75mm, so even today there's no real agreement on hook numbers. Go by the mm size if you can.

That said, this is a wonderful collection of lovely thread patterns, and would be a great addition to any thread crocheter's library. Highly recommend this classic.
Caroline can seem a bit condescending.  I didn't gain anything from the four+ hours I spent listening to it.

I'm really happy with this newest entry from Creative Haven. The artwork is very nice, with a lot of detail as well as a few with more "open" designs.

As with all Creative Haven books, this one is printed on good quality paper, on one side only, and the pages are perforated to allow for removal to make coloring easier. This also makes it easier to use any coloring medium you choose: colored pencil, alcohol markers, water-based markers, gel pens, etc. Yes, markers (especially alcohol markers) will bleed through but since there's nothing on the reverse, you needn't worry about ruining another drawing.

The choice of cities is interesting, too. There is an international representation, with cities from Europe, Asia, Australia, Africa, North and South America. Each city name is worked into the design, sometimes right up front and sometimes more subtle.

I also like "hidden object" twist: the small objects that have been hidden in the drawings -- various charms, critters, etc. They're very small, and not as easy to spot as you might think, although if you're coloring mindfully, you'll notice them. My intent is to color those with metallics (probably gel pens) as I find them.

This is another good one from Creative Haven, which is becoming one of my favorite brands of coloring books (Dover Publications). Good stuff here!
I purchased this book to study for my NC RE state exam. However, many of the answers and information are either outdated or incorrect. I would want a refund on this. It is very misleading.
Interesting

I feel like this book was a bait and switch. We were baited with images of Simon with a sword, some fighting and drama, and instead this...I dunno. It petered out quite quickly and I found myself wanting the book to END ALREADY. Nothing about this book was worth reading. Some scenes were cool, but mostly...the whole thing is an epilogue. And we're expected to read between the lines to see symbols and meanings and all sorts of stuff I didn't buy a book for. Like...come on. It could have been a lot better.

I would have liked it more if the text was not so hard to read. I found going to Google has been a better choice because I can at least size the text in the browser to be able to read it. Trying to read such a large book with a magnifying class is a pain in the you know what.

Fodor's travel guides can be a bit of a mixed bag: some are very good and some not. This is one of the good ones.

If you have room for only one travel guide for your first visit to Washington, D.C., this is the one to get. Comprehensive enough for all but frequent DC visitors or residents who already know most of the hidden treasures, this will give you what you need to know to put together a realistic tour of the nation's capital as well as surrounding points of interest (Mount Vernon, Annapolis, etc.).

I lived in the DC area for several years and although a lot has changed, there are still some things that everyone should see. You would be hard-pressed to find a more complete collection of museums -- history, art, air and space, documents, libraries -- in one relatively small area, which makes visiting here so productive.

The general organization of the book is good -- it starts with more general info: how and when to plan your trip, general layout of DC ("What's Where"), top attractions, suggested itineraries and tours, etc. From there, it delves into details -- locations, where to eat, where to stay, etc. All good stuff. An entire chapter on "Performing Arts" -- for me, that alone is worth the price of the book (that's my background). It includes the obvious venues like the Kennedy Center for the Performing Arts, Ford's Theatre (where Lincoln was shot), and the National Theatre, as well as lesser known smaller venues outside of the immediate downtown area (Arena Stage, Folger Theatre, Wolf Trap, Constitution Hall, etc.). Each chapter includes a "planning" section -- how to get tickets, how to save money, etc.

This is a seriously comprehensive guide, Very nicely done! Solid 5 stars.

Like other readers, I thought that I was going to enjoy this book because I thought that it would be like John Fowles'The French Lieutenants Woman.  However, in all honesty, I feel that this author used the vehicle to ramble on and on in order to display what she feels are great literary abilities.  This novel contains a great deal of poetry.  I have nothing against poetry, but non of the poetry is lovely or memorable.  If the poetry is not wonderful then the reader begins to wonder why is he wasting so much time reading about poets that do not really speak to him or her.  The two poets in the novel speak to each other, but are of very little interest to the reader.  The author appears to me to be very pretentious.  While the story line is interesting, the way that it is presented makes for a very boring read.  The  novel rambles on for 544 pages.  I think there was enough here for only 144 pages.  This is no French Lieutenant's Woman.  The author seems to want to prove how capable she is to the reader, but in doing so she looses her reader.  There is no passion in her writing, even though she attempts to write a passionate romance.  She attempts to appear intelectual while trying to write about passion.  It doesn't work!

I rather liked this book. It's definitely NOT a travel guide; it's more of a philosophical exploration of variously defined "places" and "locations," some of which might be visitable, but others not.

I'm a geography "junky," and I often get lost for hours exploring via Google Earth. This book was kind of like that experience, except it utilizes the written word rather than visual images. The author's method of describing these places, utilizing essay, worked fine for me. It was a bit like being in a pinball machine, each chapter a "concept" to bounce against, moving from level to level.

What this book did for me -- I will probably look at places a little differently now. I love road trips, and love traveling "out-of-the-ordinary" routes (e.g., I did a cross-country road trip some years ago from Seattle to Duluth, MN, traveling ONLY 2-lane roads). I really enjoy discovering old run-down dilapidated homesteads and barns, and trying to find out who lived there. Small towns (pop. < 500) fascinate me -- what do the people who live there do for a living? Do their children stay, or move on to "the big city"? I'm interested in history, certainly, but also in the how and why certain places exist.

So this book was a natural fit for me. A bit disjointed, sure, but with a lot of good stuff worth mining for.
book was disappointing

The book is very slow and deliberate in approach, and it’s not surprising that the end leads to nothing.  The book is about things coming to an end and memories of those things coming to an end as well. That’s it!  There is nothing else taking place here, despite what others may say in their reviews.
Jeremy Robinson always gives his readers a roaring romp!
Not very happy. It was a color book but had many freehand drawing pages and I cannot freehand draw for anything and do not find it relaxing. For me, this was not a good buy.
Very little information that can't be found online. It wasn't any more informative than TripAdvisor

One Pan, Two Plates: Vegetarian Suppers

I was provided an advance reader’s copy of this book from the publisher, Chronicle Books, for evaluation/review.

I’m not a vegetarian, but I do love vegetables, and I especially enjoy finding creative ways to optimize flavor and create main-dish meals using seasonal produce available in my part of the country (Pacific Northwest). I’m also single, so I’m especially interested in cookbooks that feature meals sized for two servings.

Bottom line: This is a cookbook worth having if you’re cooking for one or two people. The recipes are creative but not at all intimidating, even for more inexperienced cooks. With clear instructions and mostly easy-to-source ingredients, this is a cookbook that will fit a range of experience and tastes.

The book is an attractively put together paperback, easy to read, organized nicely into chapters that make sense. There are quite a few photos, but not every recipe has a picture. Measurements are given in both US and metric units. Recipes require 30 minutes OR LESS of "hands-on" time (prepping/etc.). Printing is crisp and easy to read, and the use of color print highlights rather than detracts. Overall, nice presentation.

My usual practice is to make 3 recipes to check out the clarity of instructions. I followed that practice with this book and chose three recipes that used ingredients I had on hand from a local CSA (consumer supported agriculture) delivery earlier in the week. My CSA delivers a bin of local vegetables and fruits every 2 weeks — so I always have fresh seasonal produce on hand.

First, Italian Omelet with Mushroom, Fontina, and Basil.
Yes, an omelet for supper — this one featuring mushrooms, baby spinach, onion, grape tomatoes, and fontina and Parmesan cheese. The recipe says this takes 30 minutes start to finish, and I found that accurate including the prep time (chopping, slicing, etc.). This is a traditional omelet stuffed with the mushroom/spinach/onion/cheese and topped with tomatoes & basil. Not too fancy, but VERY tasty. The fresh basil is critical — otherwise, it would be a bit too bland for my taste.
This was good, and I’ll likely make it again. Fontina is a cheese I don’t use that often and I forget how much I really like it. It definitely works in this.

Next, Roasted Brussels Sprouts with Butternut Squash, Apple, and Walnuts
Oh, YUM!! I’m crazy about Brussels sprouts anyway and I’m always looking for new ways to cook them. I do a lot of roasted vegetables, so this was a natural for me. The novelty was the addition of the apple — never did that before with Brussels sprouts, and I’m blown away by how good it is. This combination also includes butter beans and dried cranberries, seasoned with shallot, rosemary, and maple syrup. Seriously good stuff — and will likely take a turn on my Thanksgiving dinner table this year.

Finally, Bow Ties with Brussels Sprouts, Gorgonzola, and Hazelnuts
Yes, Brussels sprouts again (I had them on hand, so … ). Another winner — the combination of the gorgonzola with the toasted hazelnuts and quartered Brussels sprouts is just about perfect. Throw in bow-tie pasta, and you have a fast (20 minutes) tasty meal. Another winner.

So there it is — three winners. I saw several more recipes that I’ll be trying out over the coming weeks, too — so this book is staying on my kitchen bookshelf for the time being (I keep the bulk of my cookbook collection in several bookcases in other rooms).

Highly recommended without reservation: solid 5 stars.

There's a good variety of designs in lots of different shapes -- hexagons, squares, circles, flowers, etc. Many of these will serve nicely as jumping off points for projects. None are technically very difficult -- I'd say almost all of them could be made by advanced beginners, folks who've learned to handle a steel hook and thread.

But as with most crochet designs, these can also be made with yarn and a larger hook. Yes, it says "Thread Motifs," but don't be afraid to try them with yarn. Many of these would be wonderful as the foundation of an afghan design instead of the traditional granny square.

One tip: if you're planning to use one of these as part of a larger project (say a tablecloth, afghan, bedspread, set of placemats, etc.), make one motif in your thread (or yarn) of choice, unravel, and measure. Use that as a basis to purchase the material you'll need. I've done that for a set of placemats and coasters, and it worked perfectly.

My only quibble (and it's a small one) is that I'd like more square-shaped motifs. The round and floral designs can be fairly easily "squared" with the addition of a row or two, so it's not a huge problem.

Overall, a nice selection of motifs with good clear instructions.

I've been a fan of Alison Weir for years and have read all of her books. I've also had a fascination with this period of history, so it's a natural fit. And there's nothing I like better than being introduced to a rather obscure historical character who nevertheless was an active player in the courts of Henry, Mary, Elizabeth I, and Mary Queen of Scots.

Lady Margaret Douglas, the niece of Henry VIII, isn't mentioned very often -- there was so much going on during the years in which she lived that her story is naturally overshadowed by the more dramatic (and well-known) episodes. Still, she did play pivotal roles in the intrigues, was confined to the Tower by both Henry and his daughter, Elizabeth I, at different times, and played the political games that were necessary to survive as well as any of her more famous contemporaries.

But Margaret was also human, and this is where Weir's writing stands apart from others who take on this slice of English and Scottish history. Weir really researches her subject, and, in this case, she shows us Margaret's softer side in her own words: her letters and sonnets.

Weir's style may not be for everyone -- it can be rather information-packed, and I felt the need on occasion to make notes on post-its just to keep the players straight. But the end result is so worth it! I found myself doing the "just one more chapter" bedtime delay more than once just to see what happened next.

Kudos to Weir for her meticulous research, and for presenting such a complex character in such an approachable manner.

I first learned to crochet with thread when I was just 5 years old, taught by my mother who always had a project in the works: a bedspread, tablecloth, chair set, or doilies.

I rediscovered thread crochet in the early 1990s, and this was the first book of patterns that I purchased. I've made every doily in this book, and they all turned out absolutely beautiful. I've made several of them at least twice, using different threads, even different sizes (made one in size 30 thread to get a smaller size ... ). April's "Tiffany" pattern is a favorite of mine, as is July's "Summer Sun" (I've done it in pale creamy yellow as well as a peachy rose). December's "Snow Flurry" is stunning in pearly white "metallic" thread [[ASIN:B004WQPIOG Coats Crochet Aunt Lydia's Crochet, Cotton Metallic Size 10, White/Pearl]] (white thread with a pearlized filament), as well as basic white.

The instructions are clear and easy to follow for an advanced beginner or intermediate crocheter, and expert-level hookers will have no problems at all. The ONLY thing that would make this book better would be the inclusion of symbol charts, but Leisure Arts doesn't use those for most of their patterns.

I added the Kindle version to my library so that I can "carry" the patterns wherever I go and not have to lug around a bunch of books or brochures. The Kindle formatting is excellent -- someone took their time and did a good job making sure the photos are in the right places.

I'm currently working on June's "White Blossom," changing up the colors a bit (center flower will be made with this: [[ASIN:B0033P5112 Bulk Buy: DMC Thread Traditions Crochet Cotton Size 10 350 yds Variegated 145-5109 (3-Pack)]] - a very soft pastel variegated thread; the rest will be a soft antique white [[ASIN:B001C24E9I Coats Crochet Aunt Lydia's Crochet, Cotton Classic Size 10, Antique White]]. I'll try to add a photo when I finish.

Overall, lovely collection of doilies, perfect for the advanced beginner or more experienced crocheter. Solid 5 stars rating.

Book is strictly for someone who has never taken a Spanish class. Too many adds messes up the flow of the book. Ok for a beginner though. Not for someone who has command of Spanish and wants to improve.

There are only TEN recipes. At $9.95 + tax, that amounts to about $1.00 a recipe!

What a nice book! I absolutely love the colors of fall (my entire house is decorated in colors of gold, rust, pumpkin, sage), so this book really spoke to me!

The pictures aren't huge -- but they're positioned nicely on each page, making each a potential framable masterpiece. Lots of leaves, pumpkins/gourds, sunflowers, chrysanthemums, and some text provide ample opportunity to flex your fall-ish coloring techniques.

An added bonus: each image is shadowed, making it "pop" in an almost 3-dimensional way on each page. The paper is a fairly bright white -- I almost wish they'd gone with a more ivory tone -- and not terribly thick, but since each page is printed on one side only, you don't need to worry about bleed through ruining another image on the reverse. Just be sure you put a page or two of scrap paper behind the image you're coloring if you use markers, or just remove the page from the book before coloring.

I'm really happy with this book -- very nice designs ​and the shadow is such a nice twist. Good stuff!

I don't consider myself a "seasoned traveler," but I'm probably a bit beyond the "total novice" stage. I've done two guided/accompanied vacation tours in Europe, and one concert tour traveling with a large performing ensemble in Eastern Europe for 19 days. I've also done 5 or 6 international business trips that lasted a few days each. So, while I'm no globe-trotter, I've traveled a bit.
That said, I found a lot of the information in this book to be very basic. I didn't pick up any new insights regarding packing, wardrobe, personal safety, or health. Most of this really is common sense (drinking as much water on the plane as you can handle, packing no more than you can comfortably carry yourself, etc.). Some of the information is a little dated, too -- most airlines now count a woman's handbag as one of the two allowed carry-ons.
I _did_ find a few gems: taking along post cards of my home town and photos of my house, for example, is a lovely idea, which I will use on my next trip. I was gratified to find that Sharon's approach to jet lag is precisely what I've discovered (get yourself on the new time cycle as soon as possible, beginning the day of your departure flight, staying awake during the "wake" hours of the new time zone, and making sure you sleep during the "sleep" hours even if it means taking a mild over-the-counter sleep aid). Packing some extra "give-aways" like small candy bars is a nice touch as well.
I would have liked to have seen a bit more information on getting around once you're at your location, especially by car. The author seems to suggest that public transportation and walking are the only alternatives. This works great if you're visiting a city with public transportation. But part of the joy of travel is getting off the beaten path into the countryside. Sometimes, renting a car makes sense.
I'd recommend this book to someone who is looking forward to a second international vacation (I agree with the author that the first solo trip abroad should probably be a guided/accompanied tour), and wants to try "winging" it. If you've traveled by yourself more than a couple of times, you'll probably find this a little too basic.

I really like Frommer's travel guides. They're always full of wonderful color photographs, interesting side notes, etc. This one is no exception.

This volume provides a very nice overview, suitable for trip planning purposes, but I'm not sure useful it would be as a day-to-day in-country travel guide. In order to cover all of Italy, it's fairly hefty. I think it's a wonderful choice for planning a trip, but you'll probably want to purchase a guide specific to the area you're planning to visit to get the level of detail you'll need.

For an armchair traveler, however, or for someone in the early stages of planning a trip to Italy, it's nearly perfect.

I honestly expected more from a devotional book. The Pages are all identical. They are cute, but don’t provide scriptures or any prompts leading you to a Bible passage or even a general thought. I currently don't use essential oils much beyond as a way to add fragrance to my wax melts, or occasionally added into some DIY cleaning products. However, I do some prepping, and wanted to add to my library about various life subjects, especially including that which pertains to our health. I figure adding in a book on how to use essential oils offers another facet of of options when/if SHTF or TEOTWAWKI, or even short term crisis. I've also added in nutrition books, herbs, anatomy, other medical texts, and so on. We will need some books when there is no internet to rely on!

Some people may use it in the meantime instead of modern medicine, or in addition to. I totally get that. I think EOs can be great some applications. Did you know a popular, name brand, respected mouth wash is comprised of 3-4 essential oils?

As for this book, is it worth adding to collection? Short answer, NO. I will tell you why:

What this book does have:
This book provides a primer on essential oil use. It discusses Usage, dosage, methods of application, carrier oils, guidelines, purity, what essential oils are (their compounds, constituents), a little bit of the history, and possible contraindications (with other meds). The beginning part is informative, and makes for a worthwhile read (say, 61 pages)

What it doesn't- and this is the deal breaker:
It has lists of ailments, but doesn't describe what they are. So you might know you have toe fungus and can find the recipe of how to treat it, but some ailments you can't look up if you don't know what you have. Like, unless you happen to know what the symptoms are of emotional shock, or compartment syndrome, ecthyma, wolf parkinson white syndrome? Or maybe you have some medical background, ok, so is it Giardia or cryptosporidosis? Hypothyroid or Hashimotos? You better know the symptoms for each, as the treatments/protocol are different. etc. etc

So, NO description, NO symptoms. NO way to learn that from this book. I say it again.. there is no symptom list, and there are no symptoms listed along with how to treat the ailment. If this included a way to look up symptoms, OR if the ailments they decided to include also included a list of symptoms, it would be much more beneficial reference book.. at least in terms of some of the more basic ailments. (ie; toe fungus. acne, bruises, chapped skin, etc)

Then, even if it had a symptoms included, some of the ailments are in my uneducated guess are not all that treatable with essential oils. At least not without A LOT more information. This somewhat useless sections takes up the remainder of the book (lages 66-158)

DANGER:
This book lists treatment with essential oils for CANCER, AUTISM, ASPERGER SYNDROME, HIV, BRAIN INJURY, RABIES, and so on with no further discussion or explanation. Few drops of this and that, rub it here and there.. and... ????

There is just not enough information beyond the basic introduction.

What a disappointment! For a cookbook that claims to offer "delicious recipes that are healthy AND easy on the budget!," this book leaves a LOT to be desired.

Since when is canned chicken "frugal"? Since when is a HUGE amount of sodium "healthy"? OK, I'll give you "fast," but not THAT much faster than using fresh ingredients. Only a few dishes are seasoned with anything more than salt and pepper. Recipes using canned diced potatoes, canned sliced carrots, canned mixed veggies, canned pasta sauces, etc., and sodium well over 1000 mg per serving abound. How about a chunky tomato soup consisting of 2 jars of spaghetti sauce, a can of diced tomatoes, water, Italian seasoning, a handful of fresh kale (one of the few recipes that uses ANY fresh ingredients at all), some ground turkey, and 3 tablespoons of Spenda(?!?)? Or a "Creamy Chicken Noodle Soup" using two cans of chicken breast, canned sliced carrots, chicken broth made from bouillon, minced garlic from a jar and fresh celery, with fettucine, thickened with cornstarch. When I see a name like "Creamy Chicken Noodle Soup," I expect there to be some element of "cream" associated with it: cream, half-and-half, evaporated milk, sour cream, etc. Not this one. There are several other naming "issues" -- minestrone that contains ground beef, chowder that is really just a vegetable soup in a clear broth, etc. Many recipes call for "egg whites" -- with the suggestion to add some artificial yellow food coloring (!) to make them look more like "real" eggs. Did anyone actually bother to test these recipes? Several of the main-dish recipes call for "vanilla yogurt" combined with "Cajun seasoning," canned pasta sauce or canned tomatoes. Surely they mean *plain* yogurt??

But my biggest beef (no pun intended) with this book is the lack of quality fresh ingredients. Honestly -- fresh chicken will almost always be cheaper than canned; fresh veggies will be cheaper (and contain more nutrients) than canned (I don't have as much of an issue with frozen veggies, as they tend to be more nutrient-rich than canned). And the time to table when using fresh ingredients isn't THAT much different!

There are a number of VERY good cookbooks that address the need to create nutritious meals quickly and on a budget -- do yourself a favor and look elsewhere. ([[ASIN:076110481X Desperation Dinners]] is one that uses mostly fresh ingredients.)
Give me a break. Total waste of money.

... we'd all be in trouble!

I've been a fan of Angie Bailey's "Texts From Mittens" on Catster almost from the beginning (2 yrs), and I had already pre-ordered her first book of "Texts ... " when I was offered the chance to preview it here.

As the caretaker to two finicky kitties, I can see a lot of my own cats' attitudes in Mittens. The book is short -- barely 100 pages -- but it's filled with some of the best texts discussions from Angie's blog plus some new ones.

There's a character guide at the beginning so you'll get to know the key players in Mittens's life: Drunk Patty (the tipsy neighbor), Stumpy (Mittens best kitty friend), Phil (the "filthy beast" of a dog who shares the house with Mittens and Mom), etc. Mittens has a particular affinity for Judge Judy and is no stranger to online shopping, either.

A typical conversation between Mittens and his human:

"Stumpy ditched me for that tramp of a tabby.

-- Sorry, Mitty!

What happened to "bros before hoes"?

-- But you ditched him last week and took a nap.

"Doze before bros" is completely acceptable. #LookItUp"

--- --- ---

Mittens seems to be getting the hang of the hash-tag, too.

Really, most of these are quite clever, and definitely show the self-centered self-righteous attitude of cats, familiar to those of us who have had the pleasure of sharing our lives with them over the years. I just wish it were a little longer, more than 100 pages.

Still, this would be a wonderful gift for your cat-loving friends.

Someone whose a fan of Marfa, I actually lived on the Barrel Springs Ranch in the late 1960s, which is located about 30 miles outside of Marfa. I went my first and second grades in Marfa; and also lived in the the famous hotel Paisano for a couple of weeks while they were finishing our home on the ranch. Since, I have visited dozens of times, mostly staying in the Indian Lodge outside of Fort Davis. There are so many gorgeous landscapes and true and beautiful architecture, which I did not see in this book. I bought it for my 90-yr-old father, who was the manager of the Barrel Springs Ranch. He is the one who took me back at the many times to visit; then I started taking my son. I just don't get it. This wonderfully God-given country, and this book fails to portray the beauty. Half of it is blank, white pages with descriptions of the photo on the other page. ??? What a waste of space. This book is more for the new eclectic group of young 20 somethings. Just wasn't at all what I was expecting to find.

I have been a fan of MauindiArts on Facebook, where she has generously shared a few of her lovely designs on the Coloring Books for Adults group there, so I was thrilled when I heard that she had a book available on Amazon!

I'm really impressed! The designs are wonderful, and a great variety of patterns and styles. Some have more "wide open spaces"; others are much more detailed. There are mandalas, flowers, hamsa, even some fish. Designs are printed on one side only, so you needn't worry about ruining a design due to bleed-through. That said, if you use markers, you'll want to put a sheet or two of paper behind the design, as the ink could bleed into the next picture. The paper quality is good -- similar to all of the Creatspace-produced coloring books, and a soft white (not "pure" bright white). The pages are not perforated, but there are ways to remove them from the book that won't hurt the page (I just use an exacto knife and a ruler ... ).

There's something for everyone in this book, and I look forward to hours of coloring relaxation!

"Blue is the color of longing."

So begins the first color-related chapter of this little book, setting the tone for what is to follow. Four colors are covered: blue, red, green, and gold, each with a chapter devoted to the history and meaning of its use in western art.

The author provides examples from the 14th to the 20th centuries and explains the artists' uses and meanings of each color. The "unfolded" in the title has both literal and figurative meanings: the paintings are presented in a series of fold-out light cardstock-weight panels -- you literally unfold them to view the paintings, and the author does a good job of figuratively "unfolding" the layers of meaning within each color chapter. Each painting is described in detail, providing historical background and analysis of the featured color.

The book itself is small, making it a wonderful stocking stuffer for the budding artist or someone with an interest in art history. There is so much great info distilled into an informative small package. Solid five stars -- I loved it!
This book says it is for  0-6 months but is a paperback that could be easily torn or ruined from drool. It is not a board book or made with baby safe materials.

I've done two cruises to Alaska so far, and currently have two more booked (July 2019 and July 2020). My first ever cruise was a 14-day roundtrip from Seattle to Anchorage on a cruise line that had been recommended by friends. I had such a brilliant time I decided to make cruising to Alaska an annual "thing."

So I have some experience with cruise travel in the Pacific NW. I found this Fodor's Complete Guide to Alaska Cruises to be an excellent resource for a first-time cruise. A large portion of the book (almost 1/2) is spent detailing each individual cruise line that sails in Alaskan waters, as well as detailed descriptions of the ships used by each line, cabins, dining venues, entertainment, pricing, etc., as well as a discussion of target demographics (is this cruise line a good one for young children? Does this cruise line cater to older adults with mobility issues?). So if you're new to cruise travel in general and want to try it out on an Alaska cruise, this would be an excellent resource to start your research.

The second section of the book deals with ports of embarkation (where does the cruise start/end?) -- with details about the cruise terminal (location, what to expect, etc.) as well as the "touristy" things you need to know if you want to arrive a couple of days before your cruise and check out the sights. After that is Ports of Call -- details about all the ports in Alaska and Western Canada (i.e., Victoria) that might be included on your cruise. A section on Inland Cruise Tour Destinations -- some cruise lines combine a cruise with a "land" tour (Princess and Holland America, for example).

Overall, I really liked this book. Although I've "done Alaska" a couple of times, I learned more than a few things from this book, and I now have a few more places to explore on my upcoming trips. Highly recommended for first-time cruisers trying to plan an Alaska cruise; experienced cruisers should also find some new info that makes it worth adding another travel book to the library. Solid 4 stars - I like it.

The title implies simple cooking with “75 Modern Recipes…” That is not the case. Most people use a pressure cooker to save time, and don’t have the time, to find the fancy ingredients, that are called for in this book.

Oxtails with fennel, Osso Buco, Duck Confit, Mussels with Garlic + Lager, Spanish Garlicky Squid + Chorizo, are some of the recipes.

Reality did occasionally set-in with recipes for cooking rice, Macaroni + Cheese, Polenta, French Onion Soup, and Beef Barley Vegetable Soup.

I have worked in a hotel kitchen, and I know something about food. If I want to do fancy cooking, this book wouldn’t be the one I use.

I consider myself an "amateur" wine person -- I lived in the SF Bay area for about a dozen years and got interested in wines then. I've visited many wineries in CA, and after moving to Washington state and retiring, I did a lot of traveling which brought me into contact with a lot of wines from other areas around the world. I've kind of made it one of my life's missions to find the best wines I can for less than $20 per bottle. Yes, great wines can be had for < $20 if you know what to look for.

I'm always interested in learning more about wine -- you can learn only so much from wine tastings -- and when I saw this book, I thought it might be a good overview. Based on the reviews, I opted for the hardbound book rather than the Kindle edition.

I'm so glad I did. It's a beautifully put-together book, well-deserving of its James Beard award in 2019. It covers wine basics (characteristics, flavor notes, wine glasses, storing wine, etc.), wine and food pairing, grape varieties, and wine regions throughout the world. Each chapter is well illustrated with various types of graphs/charts, maps, and color illustrations, all of which explain what might be considered more complex concepts.

I've been experimenting with wines from New Zealand and Australia lately, so I was eager to see what the authors had to say about wines from this area. Both areas were well covered and the chapters helped "fill in" some of the gaps in my knowledge.

This was far more and far better than I expected. I like it so much that I've purchased 3 more copies to have on hand as gifts for my wine-curious friends. It works for wine beginners as well as more seasoned wine connoisseurs. Highly recommended!

I have all of the PW cookbooks and pre-ordered this one as soon as I knew it was being written. I love watching Ree and think she is great, however this book was disappointing to me. I spent time looking through the book and it took a while before I found a recipe I'd even want to try. Like others have mentioned, there's a lot of basic stuff here and it felt like I could find many of these types of recipes with an easy online search. And I'm probably unlike most here when I say that I think I'm over seeing 12 or 15 photos of each recipe. I literally have thousands of cookbooks and I LOVE photos, but I'm sorry I really don't need a photo of each ingredient being added to a mixer or pan. It seems distracting to the recipe instructions in this book. Photos of tricky instructions are great, but the mundane, unnecessary photos are bothering me LOL. I'm keeping the book but disappointed with it. If I had seen it first in a store I would not have purchased it. Sorry Ree, this one missed the mark.

Edited to add that I've decided to return this book. After going through it again I realize I will never use it.

Full disclosure: I've lived in or around Seattle since 1997, and I've made a point of exploring as much of the Puget Sound area as I can. While I wasn't born here, I feel I have a reasonably good grasp of the city's attractions and highlights. After all, I CHOSE to relocate here and I've never regretted that decision.

Even with my residential history, there are things included in this guide that are new to me (and are now on my list of places to visit!). Over the years, I've developed into a bit of a "foodie" -- I love to cook, and I especially enjoy trying new restaurants. Seattle is well-known for its food. "Top Chef" - Season 10 - was filmed in Seattle and featured various Seattle chefs, restaurants and specialties. You won't find better fresher seafood than in the Puget Sound area, and folks here know how to cook it. Just like its hard to find bad barbecue in Kansas City (my home town), it's hard to find bad seafood in Seattle. This guide will lead you to some of the very best Seattle has to offer, and there are some moderately priced options listed that are simply wonderful!

The book is nicely organized, too, both for pre-planning your visit as well as using the guide while you're traveling around the city and surrounding areas. It does a great job of outlining the various Seattle neighborhoods and their attractions (it does leave out a few but they're not as well known and don't have the concentration of restaurants/places of interest as others: Magnolia, Northgate, Greenwood and Lake City, for example.)

Restaurants do come and go here, but the recency of this book, as well as the recommendations (more "time-tested" establishments), bode well for finding a good place to eat.

As for visual attractions -- Seattle is a beautiful city with amazing vistas, mountains, water, and in the summer, GREAT weather filling longer days (sun up around 4:30-5:00am; sunset between 9:00-10:00pm). You'll find the usual array of museums, concerts, and exhibits as well as some of the "quirkier" attractions that you'll find only in Seattle.

The ONLY downside I could find here is the lack of a single removable consolidated map of the area included in the book (many of the Fodor guides include city maps). There are detailed maps for each chapter/sub-chapter showing neighborhoods, attractions, etc., but it would have been nice to include a larger all-inclusive map. This is easily rectified either electronically or at any of the gas stations/tourist stops around the city. It's not enough to deduct a star because the rest of the guide is so comprehensive.

Fodor got this one right. Solid 5 stars.

I'll admit it: I'm an adult who loves children's pop-up books. I've amassed a decent collection and enjoy seeing how different artists have played with construction techniques to enhance the story. So I was curious to see how a book about space would incorporate the various interactive elements.

From that aspect, this book is an excellent example of how to use tabs, sliders, pop-ups, windows, etc. effectively to amplify the various concepts. I had a little trouble with one of the sliders hanging up about 1/8" from the end, but that's relatively minor given how many moving parts there are in this book. The pop-ups are quite good (especially the half globe!), neatly cut, and obvious as to what action is needed to animate.

Suitable for a younger student (I'd say in the 7-10 yr old group), it contains a lot of info about a lot of things, but nothing too deep. I like the flow of the topics: starting with man on earth looking at the heavens ("The Starry Sky") to man actually traveling into space ("Space Travel") to the equipment used in space exploration ("Rockets of Yesterday and Today"), and finally an exploration of space from nearby (the space station - and getting there) to far away (the planets and galaxies, probes and satellites).

The book was originally published in France under the title "Le Grand Livre de l'Espace animé" ("The Big Book of Animated Space") so that probably explains the international focus, especially on the International Space Station pages, pointing out the country- or continent-specific contributions ("the Japanese module Kibo," "the European module Columbus," "the American module Destiny," etc.). Some of the information provided seems rather random, but some data points are clearly intended to answer questions children might have about space travel and exploration ("what does an astronaut wear under the space suit?" for example).

Is it the "ultimate" book of space? No, but I don't think it was originally intended to be. Is it a "big book"? Yes -- it measures 13" x 10" x 1.25" It covers a lot of territory in a user-friendly way. It would be a nice gift for a child interested in learning some useful fun facts about space exploration, or a good example of paper construction techniques for the adult collector. Solid 4 stars.

I purchased several Christmas coloring books this year, and this is by far my favorite. The others have their charms, for sure, but this is the one I come back to again and again.

The designs are very detailed, but not impossible to color if you are patient and take your time. Since they're printed on one side of the page only, you can use any coloring medium: alcohol markers (BIC Mark-its, Copic, Spectrum Noir, etc.); watercolor markers (Tombow, Crayola Supertips, etc.), colored pencils, gel pens, etc. Just be sure to put a piece or two of paper behind the design as you color to prevent possible bleed through to the next page.

The designs are classic shapes -- lots of stars, ornaments, trees, even a mandala or two. They're nice enough that they would be perfect framed on the wall during the holidays.

I doubt that I'll finish coloring all the designs in this book this year, so I'll still have it to work on next year, too. Really happy with this.

I've done some origami before -- from really simple to fairly complex -- so I was interested to see how two classically Japanese art forms -- manga and origami -- might intersect.

This is an interesting mix. You get instructions for creating 20 characters using a variety of folds. The head and body are the same for each character, and there are 3 basic hair styles. By mixing these with the "clothing," you get a variety of 3-D characters.

These are ADVANCED origami projects -- the head alone requires 27 steps, the body 38 steps, and each hairstyle takes 33 steps. While the clothing patterns range from beginner to expert levels, the head/body/hair would definitely be classified as "advanced." With patience, however, each pattern is doable.

A link is provided to a website where you can download jpg files for different colors and shapes of eyes, and instructions provided on how to use the files to print onto your flesh-toned origami paper. For each character, you create the head, body, and hair first; then fold the pieces of clothing. The clothing usually contains slits in which to insert the body/head pieces. All instructions are very clear, once you learn the symbols for the 6 basic folds. Still, because some of the components are very small (the designs use 4"x4", 3"x3", 2"x2" and 1"x1" squares of paper), a certain amount of dexterity is needed to successfully produce some of the pieces that make up the characters.

I liked this book and will likely make a few more of the characters -- I tested the instructions/etc. on the "shrine maiden" (p. 120) and "fairy" (p. 146) using [[ASIN:B00877ADZ0 Aitoh Origami Paper, 5.875 by 5.875-Inch, 100 Colors, 100-Pack]]. In addition to the list of tools recommended in the book, I also used a bone folder to help with creasing the paper. You may not need one, but I found that it helped.

Overall, this is a fun new addition to the origami book shelf, but I wouldn't recommend it for really young children -- probably minimum age 10 or 12 given the complexity of the designs and the need to use a sharp pointed craft knife to create the slits. Solid 4 stars.

There's a lot to like about this cookbook. With 100 menus consisting of 2 recipes each, it really does have something for every taste. And it's true to the title: every recipe pair that I've made so far came together in under 30 minutes, including the requisite prep work.

What I really like is that there is minimal use of highly-processed components (canned soup, for example) which is generally the rule in other "quick fix" recipe collections. Most of these recipes use fresh ingredients, and their simplicity (fewer ingredients) is what keeps the time in the kitchen to a minimum.

As is my practice with every cookbook I review, I chose 3 selections to try based mostly on what I had on hand in the pantry or freezer. Since this book is based on the idea of 30-minute meal preparation, I chose to prepare both recipes in each of 3 menu choices.

First up: Roast Beef and Cheddar Salad. A good basic "dinner"-type salad with some meat, salad greens (romaine lettuce in this case), tomatoes, scallions, and garbanzos (in this case, canned), with a nice basic dressing with red wine vinegar, dijon mustard, olive oil, topped with a healthy sprinkling of cheddar cheese. This is paired with Tomato-Olive Muffins -- made from scratch, with prepared pesto, chopped black olives and chopped sundried tomatoes. The salad was fairly standard -- not too special, but good -- but the muffins were EXCELLENT! You put together the muffins first, and while they're baking, assemble the salad. This made a VERY nice meal, and I'll definitely be making the muffins again for sure.

Next: Chicken Sauté with Mushrooms and Spinach paired with Honeyed Carrots. For this meal, I started the carrots first, then got to work on the chicken dish. You first cook onions, mushrooms, and chunks of skinless/boneless chicken breast, set it aside and then make a sauce of sherry, cream, sun-dried tomatoes and sage. When the sauce is reduced, you add the chicken/veggies back in and add your baby spinach. By this time, the carrots are finished, so the entire meal comes together in about 20 minutes or so. And it is lovely! Will definitely do the chicken/spinach mixture again, and probably serve it over spiralized <something> - maybe zucchini or ... carrots! Easy, tasty.

Finally, I did the Pork Medallions with Sherried Shallot Sauce and Rebel with a Cause Smashed Potatoes. The pork dish uses pork tenderloin sliced into 3/4"-thick slices that are seasoned with one of the spice mixes featured in the book (they're all good), browned, and then finished with a sherry/shallot sauce. The potatoes are again fairly standard: small red or gold potatoes cut into chunks, boiled for 15-20 minutes until tender, then mashed with a potato masher leaving some chunks, and seasoned with olive oil, chicken broth, salt and pepper. They work as a nice foil for the pork, but I have to admit this wasn't my favorite combination. It was good, but nothing special, and I already make my own "smashed potatoes" (with garlic!) that I like better.

So ... 2 out of 3 winning meals, but I see enough good recipes here to warrant a solid 4 stars.

The only truly negative I can report has to do with photos. I'm personally OK with the photos in the book --there are quite a few, but not one for every recipe, and I'm OK with that. But there's a note in the introduction that if "you're a visual person, you'll be pleased to know that there is a database of photographs of recipes in this book ... " with a URL listed. I went to that URL (a specific page on the author's blog), and there are exactly 5 photos, several of which are already included in the book. So either the URL in the book is wrong, or they haven't updated the "gallery" with the additional photos as of this writing. So if you need a photo of each recipe, you may be disappointed.

That said, I did like this book, and will likely use it again.

America's Test Kitchen has put out some stellar cookbooks in the past, fully earning five-star ratings. This one was a bit of a let-down.

Don't get me wrong; there are some very good recipes in this book, and if your cookbook library is short on pasta books, this would be a good choice. It contains a nice variety of recipes from a number of cuisines, most of them easy enough for the beginner or advanced-beginner cook (the "Pasta Plus Five" chapter is particularly good for cooks without a ton of experience). I also really like the inclusion of "Lighter Options" scattered throughout the book, identified by a light grey "tag" that extends to the page edge so it's easy to flip right to those options. "Lighter" is defined as anything under 600 calories per serving, and the nutritional info for those recipes is included at the end of the book.

So what's the problem? When I selected this book to review, I was expecting a lot of new fresh takes on pasta dishes. Instead, I got a book filled with 200 fairly standard recipes, most of which I've seen and/ or prepared before, more than a few times with some minor variations. For example, the recipe for Fettuccine Alfredo is essentially the same recipe you'll find in dozens of other books.

Why the emphasis on "Revolution" in the title? Checking the Preface, written by Christopher Kimball, head honcho of Cook's Illustrated, ATK, etc., I found this: "So what is this pasta revolution about? We start by making pasta in a skillet ..." Huh? "Pasta in a skillet" is revolutionary? Ever hear of Hamburger Helper? Ever hear of skillet Mac and Cheese? Kimball continues "Or, how about pasta casseroles that use uncooked pasta such ad No-Prep Bakes Spaghetti?" Uh ... yeah. I've been making lasagne for years using uncooked lasagne noodles, so that concept isn't particularly revolutionary, either. Other "revolutionary" concepts: whole wheat pasta, using the slow cooker for sauces, recipes that make only two servings. Sorry, Christopher -- been there, done that for YEARS.

HOWEVER, I will say that some of the "pesto" recipes are new and fresh (although if it isn't basil, pine nuts, garlic, Parmesan, and oil, I'm not I'd categorize it as "pesto"). These make quick and easy "mix-ins" that make quick and easy side dishes,

I'm giving this 4 stars because the recipes in this book are consistently good, easy to prepare, and of a nice variety of cuisines. But if you've already collected a couple of good pasta books, you'll probably find this one redundant.

This will come in especially handy for those who, like me, enjoy road trips. I've made it a point to visit as many state and national parks as I can, and having a book like this early on would have helped give me a "heads up" on specific things to look at/for.

It's the usual National Geographic quality: beautiful photography, detailed maps, easy-to-read-and-understand descriptions of the places you can visit. I love that it treats city, state, and national parks equally.

As the author, Joe Yogurst, states in the introduction: "By no means is '100 Parks, 5000 Ideas' a list of the best parks in the United States and Canada. Rather, it's a window into the wide variety of green spaces scattered across this part of the world and the multitude of things that you can do in those parks and similar areas." Yogurst encourages the reader to use the book to spark ideas for further exploration based on one's own interests.

And there is inspiration abounding here. It's easy to get lost in this book -- there's so much to see! Solid 5 stars. I love it, and plan to use it in future travels.

I've long been a fan of Lonely Planet travel guides. They're especially good at ferreting out the hidden treasures, the out-of-the-way places that are worth the effort to see. So I was curious to see what they'd do with what is essentially a picture book, something a little out of the ordinary for them.

The result is a beautiful collection of photos from every continent. You get no more than one stunning photo per page, and some are spread across two pages -- no distracting "crowding" here. The book itself is large: 10 3/4" x 14 1/4" -- a true "coffee table" book -- and includes a ribbon page marker.

I found myself wandering through the pages, "armchair traveling," as it were, to the wild places featured for each continent. What makes a place wild? It would seem that "wild" is in the eye of the beholder -- or the photographer, ​in this case, but every photo features a place that is "off the beaten path," even if not totally untouched by humans. It's easy to get drawn into the photos of places I probably will never see in person in my lifetime (Antarctica, for example).

The book comes with a partial dust jacket that is finished with a slightly "pearlized" effect. The hidden treasure, however, is inside the dust jacket. Remove it from the book, unfold it, and you have a large poster-size photo of a jaguar in a tree. Just like the wild places in the book, this is a hidden gem. Lovely touch, that.

This would be a delightful gift for any friend or family member who enjoys the discovery associated with travel -- even armchair travelers. It's beautifully finished, and would look great on any coffee table (or nightstand).

I don't want to be the negative one here, but I was disappointed. There didn't seem to be anything novel. It came across as just some big ideas that may or may not work. The authors themselves mention that they're still learning about the subject, which is understandable of course, as we are all lifelong learners, but I felt like they should have been a little further along in the learning process before writing a book. The layout of the book is like a "Diary of a Wimpy Kid" for adults. Half the book is black pages with a small quote on it, and the rest of the pages are half text and half useless drawings that do nothing to further explain what you just read. You could read it in a few hours, which is probably why there's not much to take out of it. Wow. I kind of sound like a jerk. There were a few tidbits I was able to pull out, such as, "What am I deciding for my students that they could be deciding for themselves?" Anyways. That's my two cents. Hopefully it helps.
Thought it was a more full book... not a children's book...  will be donating it to our library...

After reading this book cover to cover, I had picked up only one shortcut button sequence and no tips or tricks.  This is a good book for someone who has never picked up a digital camera or an SLR camera before (if you're used to a normal 35mm camera and need to learn both digital and SLR at once you'll be overwhelmed and this can help you).  The pictures are good and the explanation is simple enough, but if you've used a digital or an SLR you can pick most of this information up without a book.  It didn't go into anything that was difficult to understand.  I bought the book before the camera and learned nothing from it, so I can imagine that if I had the camera first I wouldn't even have bothered buying the book.
